$$\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \dfrac{\Delta d}{d} \left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}\right)$$

**Result:** **Nonlinear** output. KTU examiners expect you to note this limitation.

### Mode B: Variable Area
$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A - \Delta A)}{d} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = -\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Result:** **Linear** in area change (used in angular displacement sensors).

### Mode C: Variable Permittivity
$$\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r}$$

**Result:** **Linear** in dielectric change (used in **capacitive level sensors** for non-conductive liquids).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU "Compare & Contrast" Favorite)

| Property | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| External power | **NOT required** | Required |
| Output energy | From measurand itself | Modulates external signal |
| Examples | Thermocouple, Piezo, PV cell | Strain gauge, LVDT, RTD |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Can be very high |
| Linearity | Often nonlinear | Often linear (with design) |
| Cost | Generally lower | Higher (conditioning circuit) |
| Drift | Lower (self-referencing) | Higher (excitation drift) |
| KTU mark weight | 7-mark descriptive answers | 7-mark comparative answers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TRANSDUCER TYPES] --> B[By Energy Source]
    A --> C[By Output Signal]
    A --> D[By Operating Principle]
    A --> E[By Transduction Order]
    B --> B1[Active / Self-Generating]
    B --> B2[Passive / Externally Powered]
    B1 --> B1a[Thermocouple]
    B1 --> B1b[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    B1 --> B1c[Photovoltaic Cell]
    B1 --> B1d[Tachogenerator]
    B2 --> B2a[Strain Gauge]
    B2 --> B2b[RTD and Thermistor]
    B2 --> B2c[LVDT]
    B2 --> B2d[Capacitive Sensor]
    C --> C1[Analog e.g. LVDT]
    C --> C2[Digital e.g. Encoder]
    D --> D1[Resistive]
    D --> D2[Inductive]
    D --> D3[Capacitive]
    D --> D4[Piezoelectric]
    D --> D5[Photoelectric]
    D --> D6[Thermoelectric]
    E --> E1[Primary e.g. Bourdon tube]
    E --> E2[Secondary e.g. LVDT]
    E --> E3[Tertiary e.g. Signal conditioner]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Generic Measurement System (KTU Module 1 Recap with Module 2 Emphasis)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Primary Sensing Element] --> S[Secondary Transducer]
    S --> V[Variable Conversion Element]
    V --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> O[Output Display or Recorder]
    P -.Measurand.- M[Physical Quantity]
    style P fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The measurand (e.g., pressure) acts on the **primary element** (Bourdon tube), which mechanically displaces the **secondary transducer** (LVDT core). The LVDT converts this to a voltage, which is filtered/amplified in the **signal conditioner** and finally displayed.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Passive Resistive Transducer Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    EX[Excitation Voltage Source] --> BR[Wheatstone Bridge]
    BR --> RG[Resistive Sensor Element]
    RG --> OUT[Differential Output Voltage]
    RG -.Change in R.- STRAIN[Applied Strain or Temperature]
    BR --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    OUT --> AMP
    AMP --> ADC[ADC and Microcontroller]
    style EX fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BR fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RG fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AMP fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Operating principle:** An external **excitation** (typically 5 V DC or AC) drives a **Wheatstone bridge**. One (or more) arms contain the **resistive sensor**. When the measurand changes, the bridge becomes unbalanced, producing a **differential output** proportional to the measurand. An **instrumentation amplifier** boosts the small signal (often mV) to a usable level (0–5 V or 4–20 mA) for the **ADC**.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Active Piezoelectric Sensor Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[Applied Force or Pressure] --> P[Piezo Crystal]
    P --> Q[Generated Charge Q]
    Q --> CA[Charge Amplifier]
    CA --> V[Output Voltage proportional to F]
    V --> PROC[Signal Processing Unit]
    style F fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CA fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key contrast with passive sensors:** Notice the **absence of an excitation block** — the piezoelectric crystal *generates* its own charge, which is why it is classified as **active**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive transducers. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| External power | Not required | Required (excitation source) |
| Working principle | Direct energy conversion from measurand | Modulation of an external signal parameter |
| Output energy | Derived from measurand | Derived from external source |
| Output signal level | Usually low (mV–µV) | Can be amplified easily |

**Active examples (2 marks):**
1. **Thermocouple** — converts temperature difference directly into EMF (Seebeck effect).
2. **Piezoelectric crystal** — converts mechanical force directly into electric charge.

**Passive examples (2 marks):**
1. **Strain gauge** — changes resistance with applied strain; requires bridge excitation.
2. **LVDT** — changes mutual inductance with core displacement; requires AC excitation.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a primary transducer? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **primary transducer** (or *sensing element*) is the first stage of a measurement system that comes in **direct contact with the measurand** and produces a mechanical or physical response.

**Example:** A **Bourdon tube** in a pressure gauge.
- The high-pressure fluid enters the curved tube.
- The tube tends to straighten (mechanical displacement).
- This displacement is then converted to an electrical signal by a **secondary transducer** (e.g., LVDT).

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for example with explanation.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Classify transducers. Describe the working principle of a resistive transducer with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Transducers (3 marks):**

1. **By energy source:** Active (self-generating) vs Passive (externally powered).
2. **By output signal:** Analog vs Digital.
3. **By operating principle:** Resistive, Inductive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Photoelectric, Thermoelectric.
4. **By transduction order:** Primary, Secondary, Tertiary.
5. **By application:** Industrial, Biomedical, Automotive, Aerospace.

**Working Principle of Resistive Transducer (4 marks):**

A **resistive transducer** is a passive device whose electrical resistance changes in response to a physical measurand. The base equation is:
$$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$

Any change in $\rho$, $L$, or $A$ produces a measurable $\Delta R$. The change is detected using a **Wheatstone bridge** or **potentiometric circuit**.

**Examples:**
- **Strain Gauge:** A foil or wire pattern bonded to a structure. Under strain, the wire lengthens and thins, increasing $R$ as $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
- **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):** A platinum wire whose $\rho$ increases with temperature. $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$.
- **Thermistor:** A semiconductor device with strong negative or positive temperature coefficient. Highly sensitive but nonlinear.
- **Potentiometric transducer:** A sliding contact on a resistive element converts linear/angular displacement into a resistance ratio.
- **Photoresistor (LDR):** Resistance changes with incident light intensity.

**Working circuit:** The transducer forms one (or two) arm(s) of a **Wheatstone bridge**. Excitation $V_{ex}$ is applied; the differential output $V_o$ is proportional to $\Delta R$:
$$V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the construction and working of an LVDT. State its advantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** consists of:
- One **primary coil** (P) wound at the center of a hollow cylindrical former.
- **Two identical secondary coils** (S1 and S2) wound on either side, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** (typically nickel-iron) that slides inside the former.
- The entire assembly is housed in a shielded, sealed casing.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The primary is energized with an **AC excitation** (typically 1–10 kHz, 5 V RMS).
- The two secondaries produce EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$.
- **Null position:** Core centered. $E_1 = E_2$, so net $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2 = 0$.
- **Core displaced upward by $x$:** $E_1 > E_2$, net $V_{out}$ has a **phase** indicating direction.
- **Core displaced downward by $x$:** $E_1 < E_2$, net $V_{out}$ has the **opposite phase**.
- The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ is **linearly proportional** to displacement (within the linear range).

$$V_{out} = K \cdot x \quad \text{where } K = 2 \omega I_p M / \text{unit displacement}$$

**Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Infinite resolution** (limited only by detector electronics).
2. **High linearity** (typically ±0.1% of full scale).
3. **Frictionless measurement** (no physical contact with moving parts).
4. **Robust and reliable** — ideal for harsh industrial environments.
5. Bidirectional — phase of output indicates direction of displacement.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the principle of operation of a piezoelectric transducer. List its applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (5 marks):**
The **piezoelectric effect** was discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880. Certain crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT, tourmaline) generate an **electric charge** on their surfaces when subjected to **mechanical stress**.

The governing equation is:
$$Q = d \cdot F$$
where:
- $Q$ = generated charge (Coulombs)
- $d$ = piezoelectric charge constant (C/N or pC/N)
- $F$ = applied force (Newtons)

**Key facts to write in the KTU answer:**
- It is an **active transducer** (no external power needed).
- The relationship is **linear**.
- The effect is **reversible** — applying a voltage causes mechanical deformation (*inverse piezoelectric effect*, used in ultrasonic transducers and buzzers).
- The output is **AC-coupled** (no DC response to static force). Suitable for **dynamic** measurements only (vibration, pressure fluctuations).

**Typical charge constant values:**
- Quartz: $d_{11} = 2.3$ pC/N
- PZT: $d_{33} \approx 300$ pC/N (much more sensitive)

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Accelerometers** (vibration measurement in machines, smartphones, airbags).
2. **Microphones** (sound pressure → voltage).
3. **Ultrasonic transducers** (medical imaging, sonar, NDT).
4. **Pressure sensors** in engines and hydraulic systems.
5. **Force sensors** in precision balances.
6. **Spark generators** in gas lighters.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare RTD, Thermocouple, and Thermistor. State two merits of each.**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | RTD | Thermocouple | Thermistor |
|----------|-----|--------------|------------|
| **Principle** | Resistance change of metal (PT) with T | Seebeck EMF at junction of dissimilar metals | Resistance change of semiconductor with T |
| **Active/Passive** | Passive | Active | Passive |
| **Range (°C)** | -200 to +850 | -270 to +2300 | -100 to +300 |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate (~0.4 Ω/°C for PT100) | Low (~40 µV/°C for Type K) | Very high (~100 Ω/°C) |
| **Linearity** | Excellent | Moderate (needs linearization) | Poor (exponential) |
| **Accuracy** | High (±0.1°C) | Moderate (±1–2°C) | Moderate |
| **Cost** | High | Low (rugged) | Low |
| **Stability** | Excellent | Good | Moderate (drift) |
| **Self-heating** | Issue (needs low excitation) | None | Significant issue |
| **Response time** | Moderate | Fast | Fast |

**Merits of each (2 marks each):**

**RTD (2 marks):**
1. High accuracy and excellent linearity.
2. Excellent long-term stability; ideal as a calibration standard.

**Thermocouple (2 marks):**
1. Very wide temperature range and rugged construction.
2. Fast response time; works in harsh/vibrating environments.

**Thermistor (2 marks):**
1. Very high sensitivity — large resistance change per °C.
2. Low cost and small size; ideal for consumer electronics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in "Types of Transducers" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "sensor" with "transducer":** A *sensor* detects a physical quantity; a *transducer* converts energy forms. They overlap but are not identical — write both terms in your answer to be safe.
>
> 2. **Writing "RTD" without spelling out "Resistance Temperature Detector":** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for undefined abbreviations.
>
> 3. **Stating LVDT is "active":** It is **passive** (requires AC excitation). This is one of the most common mistakes — KTU examiners specifically test this.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the working equation** when describing a transducer. Always include **at least one formula** (e.g., $\Delta R / R = G_f \varepsilon$ for strain gauge, $Q = dF$ for piezoelectric). A purely descriptive 7-mark answer without any equation typically scores **4–5 marks maximum**.
>
> 5. **Not drawing a neat labeled diagram.** In 7-mark questions, a diagram carries **at least 2 marks**. Always include a labeled block diagram or schematic.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the piezoelectric constants.** $d$ is in **pC/N**, $g$ is in **mV·m/N**. The KTU paper often gives you one and asks for the other.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Active vs Passive:** Active = self-generating (thermocouple, piezo, PV). Passive = needs excitation (strain gauge, LVDT, RTD, capacitive).
- **Gauge Factor:** $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C$. For metals $\approx 2$; for semiconductors $\approx 100$–$200$.
- **RTD equation:** $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$ with $\alpha_{Pt} = 0.00385/°C$.
- **LVDT output:** $V_{out} = Kx$. Phase of output indicates **direction** of displacement. Series-opposed secondaries.
- **Capacitive sensor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. Variable-area and variable-$\varepsilon_r$ modes are **linear**; variable-gap mode is **nonlinear**.
- **Piezoelectric:** $Q = dF$. **Active**, AC-coupled, suitable for **dynamic** measurements only.
- **Thermocouple:** $V = S(T) \cdot \Delta T$. Type K (Chromel-Alumel) is the industrial workhorse.
- **Wheatstone bridge** is the standard signal extraction circuit for resistive transducers; differential output $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.
- **Primary transducer** = direct contact with measurand. **Secondary** = converts primary's output to electrical signal.
- **Always** include at least one **equation** and one **labeled diagram** in any 7-mark answer.

### Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Scheme
> **"Definition (1 m) + Diagram (2 m) + Working (2 m) + Equation (1 m) + Application (1 m) = 7 marks"**

Master this template, and you will clear every Part-B question on transducer types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring circuits and applications of Resistance transducers: Potentiometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEIT304 - SENSORS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measuring circuits and applications of Resistance transducers: Potentiometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Measuring Circuits and Applications of Resistance Transducers: The Potentiometer

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A **Potentiometer** is a passive, variable-resistance electromechanical transducer that converts **linear displacement** or **angular rotation** into a proportional change in electrical resistance, and consequently into a proportional output voltage. It functions as an **adjustable voltage divider** consisting of a uniform resistance element with end terminals and a movable sliding contact (the *wiper*).

In the KTU 2024 framework, a potentiometer used as a transducer is classified under **Resistive (Variable) Transducers** in Module 2, alongside strain gauges and RTDs. It belongs to the family of **passive transducers** because it requires an external excitation voltage to produce an output.

### 1.2 Constructional Anatomy

A potentiometer transducer has three fundamental parts:

1. **The Resistance Element** — A uniform resistive track made of:
   - *Wire-wound* (constantan, nichrome) — high precision, low noise, but discrete resolution
   - *Carbon film* — smooth, infinite resolution, moderate life
   - *Cermet / Conductive plastic* — long life, high resolution, low noise
2. **The Wiper (Slider)** — A movable mechanical contact that taps off a portion of the total resistance.
3. **The Terminals** — Two fixed end terminals (A, B) and one wiper terminal (W).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The relationship between mechanical displacement $x$ and the tapped resistance $R_x$ is **linear** in an ideal potentiometer. This linearity is the cornerstone of its use as a calibrated measuring instrument.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Potentiometer as a "Water Tap on a Pipe":**
> Imagine a long horizontal water pipe filled uniformly with sand (the resistance element). The pipe is connected to a water pump (the excitation voltage). Now imagine a thin rod (the wiper) pushed into the pipe from above — the deeper you push the rod, the less sand remains in front of it, so the water flow (current) on the other side changes. The position of the rod is your mechanical input, and the water flow on the exit side is your electrical output. **Push the rod further = change the output proportionally.** That is exactly how a potentiometer transducer behaves.

### 1.4 Classification of Potentiometric Transducers

| **Type** | **Mechanical Input** | **Typical Application** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear (Translational) Potentiometer** | Straight-line displacement $x$ | Position of a slider, hydraulic piston, float level |
| **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometer** | Rotational angle $\theta$ | Shaft position, joystick, valve opening |
| **Multi-turn Potentiometer** | Rotational angle (multiple turns) | Precision trimming, servo feedback |
| **Wire-wound Potentiometer** | Any of the above | High-power, high-precision laboratory use |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle — The Voltage Divider Rule

When a stable DC excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across the full resistance $R$ of the potentiometer (between terminals A and B), the resistance track acts as a **two-resistor voltage divider**. The wiper splits the total resistance $R$ into two parts:

$$
R_1 = \text{Resistance from terminal A to wiper W}
$$
$$
R_2 = \text{Resistance from wiper W to terminal B}
$$
$$
R_1 + R_2 = R
$$

The output voltage $V_o$ is measured between the wiper W and one of the end terminals. Under **ideal (no-load)** conditions, the voltage divider rule gives:

$$
V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R}
$$

## 2.2 Linear Potentiometer — Displacement Equation

For a linear potentiometer of total length $L$ and total resistance $R$ with the wiper displaced by a distance $x$ from terminal B:

$$
R_2 = \frac{x}{L} \cdot R
$$

Therefore, the **no-load output voltage** is directly proportional to displacement:

$$
V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}
$$

This is a beautifully **linear** relationship between $x$ and $V_o$, which is the principal reason potentiometers are favored for displacement measurement.

## 2.3 Sensitivity — The "Why" Behind the Slope

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity** is defined as the rate of change of output voltage with respect to the mechanical input (displacement or angle). It is the **slope of the transfer characteristic**.

For a linear potentiometer:

$$
S = \frac{dV_o}{dx} = \frac{V_s}{L} \quad \text{[V/m]}
$$

For a rotary potentiometer with full angular range $\alpha_{max}$:

$$
S = \frac{dV_o}{d\theta} = \frac{V_s}{\alpha_{max}} \quad \text{[V/rad]}
$$

**Why does this matter?** Higher sensitivity means a smaller mechanical input produces a larger, more easily measurable electrical output — improving resolution and signal-to-noise ratio.

## 2.4 The Loading Effect — The Practical Reality

In real circuits, the output is never truly *no-load*. A voltmeter, amplifier, or ADC always presents a finite input resistance $R_L$. This $R_L$ appears in **parallel** with $R_2$ (the lower half of the divider), and the loaded output voltage becomes:

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2 \,\|\, R_L}{R_1 + (R_2 \,\|\, R_L)}
$$

where $R_2 \,\|\, R_L$ denotes the parallel combination:

$$
R_2 \,\|\, R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}
$$

**The Consequence:** $V_{o,\text{loaded}} < V_{o,\text{no-load}}$, causing a **nonlinearity** and a systematic **loading error** that is most severe near mid-stroke. The error is minimized when $R_L \gg R$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students forget that the loading effect is *position-dependent* — it is **zero** at the ends ($x=0$ and $x=L$) and **maximum** at $x = L/2$. Drawing a bell-shaped error curve earns you a 2-mark bonus.

## 2.5 Resolution of Wire-Wound Potentiometers

A wire-wound pot is made of discrete turns of resistance wire. The wiper can only step from turn to turn, so there is a finite **resolution**:

$$
\text{Resolution (\%)} = \frac{1}{N} \times 100
$$

where $N$ is the total number of wire turns. For $N = 200$ turns, resolution = **0.5 %** of full scale.

Non-wire-wound (carbon, cermet) potentiometers have **theoretically infinite resolution** because the resistance varies continuously.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of potentiometer numerical problems in KTU ESE.**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Tapped resistance (linear) | $R_2 = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot R$ | $\Omega$ | $x$ = displacement from one end |
| 2 | No-load output voltage | $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | V | Linear transfer characteristic |
| 3 | Sensitivity (linear) | $S = \dfrac{V_s}{L}$ | V/m or V/mm | Slope of transfer curve |
| 4 | Sensitivity (rotary) | $S = \dfrac{V_s}{\alpha_{max}}$ | V/rad or V/deg | For angular transducer |
| 5 | Parallel combination | $R_2 \Vert R_L = \dfrac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L}$ | $\Omega$ | Used in loading analysis |
| 6 | Loaded output voltage | $V_{o,L} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2 \Vert R_L}{R_1 + R_2 \Vert R_L}$ | V | Always $\leq V_{o,\text{no-load}}$ |
| 7 | Absolute loading error | $\Delta V = V_{o,\text{no-load}} - V_{o,\text{loaded}}$ | V | Position-dependent |
| 8 | Percentage loading error | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{o,\text{no-load}}} \times 100$ | \% | Maximum near mid-stroke |
| 9 | Resolution (wire-wound) | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100$ | \% | Discrete steps of wiper |
| 10 | Maximum loading error bound | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{R}{4 R_L} \times 100$ | \% | Occurs at $x = L/2$, valid when $R_L \gg R$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Loaded Output Voltage Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Starting from first principles (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law and Ohm's Law), derive the exact expression for $V_{o,\text{loaded}}$ of a potentiometer feeding a load resistance $R_L$.

### Step 1 — Define the Circuit Topology

The potentiometer with wiper at displacement $x$ presents two resistances:

- $R_1$ — upper segment (terminal A to wiper W), where $R_1 = R - R_2$
- $R_2$ — lower segment (wiper W to terminal B), where $R_2 = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot R$

A load resistance $R_L$ is connected between W and B (in parallel with $R_2$). The excitation $V_s$ is applied between A and B.

### Step 2 — Combine $R_2$ and $R_L$ in Parallel

The lower segment and the load form a parallel network. By definition of parallel resistance:

$$
R_p = R_2 \Vert R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule

The circuit now reduces to a simple series combination of $R_1$ and $R_p$ across the source $V_s$. The output voltage is the voltage across $R_p$:

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_1 + R_p}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $R_p$ and $R_1$ in Terms of $x$, $L$, and $R$

Let the fractional displacement be $k = \dfrac{x}{L}$, so that $R_2 = k R$ and $R_1 = (1 - k) R$. Substituting:

$$
R_p = \frac{k R \cdot R_L}{k R + R_L} = \frac{k R R_L}{k R + R_L}
$$

The loaded output voltage becomes:

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{\dfrac{k R R_L}{k R + R_L}}{(1 - k) R + \dfrac{k R R_L}{k R + R_L}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify by Multiplying Numerator and Denominator by $(k R + R_L)$

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{k R R_L}{(1 - k) R (k R + R_L) + k R R_L}
$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$
(1 - k) R (k R + R_L) = (1 - k) k R^2 + (1 - k) R R_L
$$

Adding $k R R_L$:

$$
\text{Denominator} = (1 - k) k R^2 + (1 - k) R R_L + k R R_L = (1 - k) k R^2 + R R_L
$$

### Step 6 — The Final Closed-Form Expression

$$
\boxed{\, V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{k R R_L}{(1 - k) k R^2 + R R_L} = \frac{V_s \cdot k}{k(1 - k)\dfrac{R}{R_L} + 1} \,}
$$

where $k = \dfrac{x}{L}$ is the fractional displacement.

### Step 7 — Verify the Two Limiting Cases

**Case (a) — No load** ($R_L \to \infty$, so $R/R_L \to 0$):

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} \to \frac{V_s \cdot k}{0 + 1} = V_s k = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} = V_{o,\text{no-load}} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Case (b) — Short-circuit load** ($R_L \to 0$): The denominator diverges $\Rightarrow V_{o,\text{loaded}} \to 0$, which is physically correct (the wiper is shorted to terminal B).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Loading Error (at Mid-Stroke)

The **absolute loading error** is:

$$
\Delta V(k) = V_{o,\text{no-load}} - V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s k - \frac{V_s k}{k(1 - k)\dfrac{R}{R_L} + 1}
$$

Factoring $V_s k$:

$$
\Delta V(k) = V_s k \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{k(1 - k)\dfrac{R}{R_L} + 1} \right] = \frac{V_s k \cdot k(1 - k) \dfrac{R}{R_L}}{k(1 - k)\dfrac{R}{R_L} + 1}
$$

The **percentage error** is:

$$
\varepsilon(k) = \frac{\Delta V(k)}{V_{o,\text{no-load}}} \times 100 = \frac{k(1 - k)\dfrac{R}{R_L}}{k(1 - k)\dfrac{R}{R_L} + 1} \times 100
$$

To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to $k$ and set it to zero. The function $f(k) = k(1 - k)$ is a parabola with maximum at $k = \dfrac{1}{2}$ (mid-stroke). Substituting $k = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{4}
$$

Assuming $R_L \gg R$ so that $k(1-k) \dfrac{R}{R_L} \ll 1$, the denominator is approximately $1$, giving:

$$
\boxed{\, \varepsilon_{max} \approx \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{R}{R_L} \times 100 = \frac{R}{4 R_L} \times 100 \,\%}
$$

**This is a high-yield KTU result** — the maximum percentage loading error of a potentiometer is approximately $\dfrac{R}{4 R_L} \times 100\%$, occurring at mid-stroke.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU 2024 Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (matches Q1(b) of the question bank in Section 5):**
> A linear potentiometer has $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_s = 10\ \text{V}$, $L = 100\ \text{mm}$, $x = 60\ \text{mm}$, and $R_L = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Fractional displacement:**

$$
k = \frac{x}{L} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6
$$

**Step 2 — No-load output voltage:**

$$
V_{o,\text{no-load}} = V_s \cdot k = 10 \times 0.6 = 6\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity:**

$$
S = \frac{V_s}{L} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.1\ \text{V/mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Lower segment resistance:**

$$
R_2 = k R = 0.6 \times 10{,}000 = 6{,}000\ \Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Upper segment resistance:**

$$
R_1 = (1 - k) R = 0.4 \times 10{,}000 = 4{,}000\ \Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_L$:**

$$
R_p = \frac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{6{,}000 \times 100{,}000}{6{,}000 + 100{,}000} = \frac{6 \times 10^8}{106{,}000} \approx 5{,}660.38\ \Omega
$$

**Step 7 — Loaded output voltage:**

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_1 + R_p} = 10 \times \frac{5{,}660.38}{4{,}000 + 5{,}660.38} = 10 \times \frac{5{,}660.38}{9{,}660.38} \approx 5.86\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 8 — Loading error:**

$$
\Delta V = 6 - 5.86 = 0.14\ \text{V}
$$
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{0.14}{6} \times 100 \approx 2.33\ \%
$$

**Step 9 — Verify with the maximum-error formula:**

$$
\varepsilon_{max} \approx \frac{R}{4 R_L} \times 100 = \frac{10{,}000}{4 \times 100{,}000} \times 100 = 2.5\ \% \quad \text{(theoretical bound, mid-stroke)}
$$

Our computed $2.33\%$ is consistent (it is below the $2.5\%$ theoretical max because $k = 0.6 \ne 0.5$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Potentiometric Measurement System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **signal-flow architecture** from mechanical input to electrical readout, including all key functional blocks, alternative construction options, and downstream application families. This is the diagram you should redraw in your KTU answer script for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Mechanical Domain]
        M1[Linear Displacement x in mm]
        M2[Angular Rotation theta in deg]
        M3[Pressure via Bourdon Tube]
        M4[Liquid Level via Float Arm]
        M5[Acceleration via Seismic Mass]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Potentiometer Transducer Core]
        R1[Resistance Element R]
        R2[Wiper Contact at Position k]
        R3[Two End Terminals A and B]
        R1 --- R2
        R2 --- R3
    end

    subgraph CONST[Construction Variants]
        C1[Wire Wound: High Precision]
        C2[Carbon Film: Infinite Resolution]
        C3[Cermet: Long Life, Low Noise]
        C4[Conductive Plastic: Smooth Output]
    end

    subgraph EXC[Excitation and Conditioning]
        E1[DC Excitation Source Vs]
        E2[Voltage Divider Action: Vo is proportional to k]
        E3[Loading Stage: Load Resistor RL]
        E4[Buffer Amplifier for RL greater than R]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Electrical Output Domain]
        O1[Analog Voltage Vo in volts]
        O2[ADC Conversion Stage]
        O3[Microcontroller Readout]
        O4[Display and Indicator]
    end

    M1 --> CORE
    M2 --> CORE
    M3 --> M1
    M4 --> M1
    M5 --> M1

    CONST -.-> R1

    CORE --> E2
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

## 4.2 Schematic Symbol and Circuit Topology (Text-Equivalent)

```
   Excitation Vs
       +
       |
       A======[=====R1=====]========W
       |                       |     |
       |                      [R2]   |  <-- Wiper
       |                       |     |
       B=========================B   |
                                      |
                              +-------+-------+
                              |               |
                            [RL]            [Vo]
                              |               |
                              +-------+-------+
                                      |
                                     GND
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- A–B: Excitation terminals across total resistance $R = R_1 + R_2$.
- W: Wiper terminal, tapping voltage proportional to $R_2$.
- $R_L$: Load resistance (voltmeter, amplifier, or ADC input) appearing between W and B.
- $V_o$: Output voltage measured between W and B (or W and A, depending on convention).

## 4.3 Transfer Characteristic Curve (Loading Effect Visual)

The **no-load curve** is a perfect straight line from $(0, 0)$ to $(L, V_s)$. The **loaded curve** sags below it, with maximum deviation at the **mid-stroke point** $x = L/2$. The deviation is zero at both ends. This is the classic "**bow-tie**" or "**S-curve sag**" shape that examiners love to draw and ask you to identify.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**No-load = straight, Loaded = sagging belly**" — the loading effect makes the potentiometer output curve bow *downward* in the middle, creating nonlinearity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Sensitivity Definition
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define sensitivity of a potentiometer transducer. State the factors on which the sensitivity depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The sensitivity of a potentiometer is defined as the rate of change of output voltage with respect to the mechanical input (displacement or rotation). For a linear potentiometer:

$$
S = \frac{dV_o}{dx} = \frac{V_s}{L}
$$

For a rotary potentiometer:

$$
S = \frac{dV_o}{d\theta} = \frac{V_s}{\alpha_{max}}
$$

**Sensitivity depends on:**
1. The magnitude of the excitation voltage $V_s$ (larger $V_s \Rightarrow$ higher $S$).
2. The full-scale mechanical range ($L$ for linear, $\alpha_{max}$ for rotary) — smaller range $\Rightarrow$ higher $S$.

---

### Question A2 — Loading Effect Explanation
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
> **Explain the term "loading effect" in a potentiometer. Why is it maximum at mid-stroke?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The loading effect refers to the reduction in output voltage that occurs when a finite load resistance $R_L$ (voltmeter, amplifier input) draws current from the wiper. This causes the output to deviate from the ideal no-load voltage divider value.

**Why maximum at mid-stroke:** At $x = 0$ or $x = L$, the wiper is at an end terminal, so the output voltage is $0$ or $V_s$ regardless of $R_L$ — the error is zero. At mid-stroke ($x = L/2$), the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_L$ is maximally reduced from $R_2$, and the voltage divider is most disturbed. The error is a quadratic function of $k = x/L$ that peaks at $k = 0.5$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **linear potentiometer transducer**. Derive the expression for its sensitivity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A linear potentiometer of total resistance **$10\ \text{k}\Omega$** is connected across a **$10\ \text{V}$** DC supply. The total length of the potentiometer is **$100\ \text{mm}$**. If the wiper is at **$60\ \text{mm}$** from one end, find:
> - (i) The output voltage under no-load.
> - (ii) The sensitivity of the potentiometer.
> - (iii) The output voltage when a load of **$100\ \text{k}\Omega$** is connected across the output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Construction:** A linear potentiometer consists of a resistance element of total resistance $R$ and total length $L$, fitted with a sliding wiper. It has three terminals: two end terminals (A, B) and the wiper terminal (W). The resistance element may be wire-wound or made of carbon film/cermet. The wiper is mechanically coupled to the moving body whose displacement is to be measured.

**Working Principle:** When a stable DC voltage $V_s$ is applied across A and B, the resistance element acts as a voltage divider. As the wiper moves linearly by a distance $x$ from terminal B, the resistance between W and B becomes $R_2 = (x/L) \cdot R$. The no-load output voltage is:

$$
V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R} = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Sensitivity derivation:**

$$
S = \frac{dV_o}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}\right) = \frac{V_s}{L}
$$

Sensitivity is **constant** and depends only on $V_s$ and $L$. A higher $V_s$ or smaller $L$ gives higher sensitivity. **[7 marks: Construction (2) + Working (2) + Derivation (3)]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Given:** $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_s = 10\ \text{V}$, $L = 100\ \text{mm}$, $x = 60\ \text{mm}$, $R_L = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**(i) No-load output voltage:**

$$
V_{o,\text{no-load}} = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10 \times \frac{60}{100} = \mathbf{6\ \text{V}}
$$

**(ii) Sensitivity:**

$$
S = \frac{V_s}{L} = \frac{10}{100} = \mathbf{0.1\ \text{V/mm}}
$$

**(iii) Loaded output voltage:**

First, the lower segment resistance:

$$
R_2 = \frac{x}{L} \cdot R = 0.6 \times 10{,}000 = 6{,}000\ \Omega
$$
$$
R_1 = R - R_2 = 4{,}000\ \Omega
$$

Parallel combination with the load:

$$
R_p = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{6{,}000 \times 100{,}000}{6{,}000 + 100{,}000} = 5{,}660.38\ \Omega
$$

Loaded output voltage:

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_1 + R_p} = 10 \times \frac{5{,}660.38}{4{,}000 + 5{,}660.38} = \mathbf{5.86\ \text{V}}
$$

**Loading error:** $\Delta V = 6 - 5.86 = 0.14\ \text{V}$, i.e., $\approx 2.33\%$.

**[7 marks: 1 mark per sub-part + 1 mark for loading calculation + 1 mark for error analysis]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the different **types of potentiometer transducers** (linear, rotary, wire-wound vs non-wire-wound) with their **characteristic features and applications**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rotary potentiometer of **$5\ \text{k}\Omega$** resistance is connected across a **$24\ \text{V}$** DC supply. Calculate:
> - (i) The output voltage when the wiper is rotated to **$45°$**, given the total angular range is **$270°$**.
> - (ii) The percentage loading error when a measuring instrument of **$50\ \text{k}\Omega$** input resistance is connected.
> - (iii) The maximum possible percentage error at any position. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Types of Potentiometers:**

1. **Linear (Translational) Potentiometer:** The wiper moves along a straight resistance track. The displacement-to-voltage relationship is linear: $V_o = V_s \cdot (x/L)$. **Applications:** hydraulic piston position, float-based liquid level, large structural displacement.

2. **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometer:** The wiper rotates about a shaft. $V_o = V_s \cdot (\theta/\alpha_{max})$. **Applications:** valve position, throttle angle, joystick, robotic arm angle.

3. **Wire-wound Potentiometer:** Made of discrete turns of resistance wire on a former. **Pros:** high power handling, low temperature coefficient, high precision. **Cons:** finite resolution, mechanical wear, noise at wiper transitions. **Applications:** precision laboratory standards, servo feedback.

4. **Non-wire-wound (Carbon/Cermet/Conductive Plastic):** Continuous resistance film. **Pros:** infinite resolution, smoother output, longer life. **Cons:** higher temperature coefficient, lower power. **Applications:** audio volume controls, consumer electronics, instrumentation.

5. **Multi-turn Potentiometer:** Helical track requiring 3–10 turns for full range. **Pros:** very high resolution. **Applications:** precision trimming potentiometers ("trimpots").

**[7 marks: ~1.4 marks per type with examples]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Given:** $R = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$, $\theta = 45°$, $\alpha_{max} = 270°$, $R_L = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**(i) No-load output voltage:**

$$
V_{o,\text{no-load}} = V_s \cdot \frac{\theta}{\alpha_{max}} = 24 \times \frac{45}{270} = \mathbf{4\ \text{V}}
$$

**(ii) Percentage loading error:**

Lower segment resistance:

$$
R_2 = \frac{45}{270} \times 5{,}000 = \frac{1}{6} \times 5{,}000 = 833.33\ \Omega
$$
$$
R_1 = 5{,}000 - 833.33 = 4{,}166.67\ \Omega
$$

Parallel combination with $R_L = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$
R_p = \frac{833.33 \times 50{,}000}{833.33 + 50{,}000} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^7}{50{,}833.33} = 819.67\ \Omega
$$

Loaded output voltage:

$$
V_{o,\text{loaded}} = 24 \times \frac{819.67}{4{,}166.67 + 819.67} = 24 \times \frac{819.67}{4{,}986.34} = 3.946\ \text{V}
$$

Absolute error:

$$
\Delta V = 4 - 3.946 = 0.054\ \text{V}
$$

Percentage error at $45°$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{0.054}{4} \times 100 \approx \mathbf{1.35\ \%}
$$

**(iii) Maximum possible percentage error (at $90°$, mid-stroke of $270°$):**

Wait — mid-stroke is at $\theta = 135°$ (half of $270°$). The maximum percentage error formula:

$$
\varepsilon_{max} \approx \frac{R}{4 R_L} \times 100 = \frac{5{,}000}{4 \times 50{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{2.5\ \%}
$$

**[7 marks: 2 marks (i) + 3 marks (ii) + 2 marks (iii)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $R_1$ and $R_2$:** Always define which end is the wiper-closer end. Label your diagram clearly. Many students lose 2 marks for an unlabeled schematic.
> 2. **Forgetting the parallel step:** A surprising number of students compute $V_{o,\text{loaded}} = V_s \cdot R_2/R$ without combining $R_2$ with $R_L$ in parallel. This is the most common error — and the most heavily penalized.
> 3. **Wrong mid-stroke assumption:** The mid-stroke is $x = L/2$ (linear) or $\theta = \alpha_{max}/2$ (rotary), **not** the angle in degrees divided by 360°. Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **No units in the final answer:** Always report $V_o$ in volts, $S$ in V/mm (or V/rad), and $\varepsilon$ in percent. Missing units cost 1 mark in the KTU scheme.
> 5. **Ignoring the resolution of wire-wound pots:** If the question mentions "wire-wound" and asks about precision, mention resolution = $1/N$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam, tick every box below — these are the absolute must-knows for the Potentiometer topic under KTU 2024 Module 2.**

### Core Concepts
- [x] A potentiometer is a **passive variable-resistance transducer** with three terminals (A, B, W).
- [x] It works on the **voltage divider principle** and converts displacement/angle into voltage.
- [x] The transfer characteristic $V_o = V_s \cdot (x/L)$ is **linear** under no-load.

### Key Formulas (memorize verbatim)
- [x] $R_2 = (x/L) \cdot R$ — fractional resistance
- [x] $V_{o,\text{no-load}} = V_s \cdot (x/L)$ — ideal output
- [x] $S = V_s / L$ — sensitivity (linear), $S = V_s / \alpha_{max}$ — sensitivity (rotary)
- [x] $R_p = R_2 \cdot R_L / (R_2 + R_L)$ — parallel combination for loading
- [x] $\varepsilon_{max} \approx (R / 4 R_L) \times 100\ \%$ — maximum loading error
- [x] Resolution (wire-wound) = $100 / N\ \%$ — discrete wiper steps

### Engineering Insights
- [x] **Loading effect** is *zero* at the ends and *maximum* at mid-stroke ($k = 0.5$).
- [x] **Wire-wound** pots have **finite resolution**; **non-wire-wound** have **infinite resolution**.
- [x] Higher excitation voltage $\Rightarrow$ higher sensitivity, but also higher self-heating.
- [x] A **buffer amplifier** (op-amp voltage follower) eliminates loading error entirely.

### Applications Snapshot
- [x] Linear/angular position sensing
- [x] Pressure (with Bourdon tube coupling)
- [x] Liquid level (float + linear pot)
- [x] Acceleration (seismic mass + pot)
- [x] Joystick and human-input devices

### Common Pitfalls
- [x] Always combine $R_2$ and $R_L$ in **parallel** before applying the voltage divider rule.
- [x] Never confuse **mid-stroke** ($k = 0.5$) with **mid-resistance** — they are the same point only for linear pots.
- [x] Always quote **units** ($V$, $V/\text{mm}$, $\%$, $\Omega$) — KTU strictly enforces this.

> **End of Notes — Potentiometer (Module 2, PBEIT304)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain gauge (gauge factor derivation), Strain gauge Load Cell, Resistance thermometer (2 wire, 3 wire & 4 wire), Thermistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEIT304 - SENSORS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain gauge (gauge factor derivation), Strain gauge Load Cell, Resistance thermometer (2 wire, 3 wire & 4 wire), Thermistor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strain Gauge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical displacement (strain) into a change in electrical resistance. It operates on the principle that the resistance of a conductor changes when subjected to mechanical deformation (tension or compression).

- **Unit of Strain (ε):** Dimensionless (m/m), often expressed in **microstrain (µε = 10⁻⁶)**.
- **Standard Gauge Length:** Typically **1 mm to 100 mm** depending on application.
- **Common Material:** Constantan (Copper-Nickel alloy, 60% Cu, 40% Ni) due to low temperature coefficient.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a rubber band being stretched. As you pull it longer, it gets thinner. If you pass electricity through it, the longer and thinner path makes it harder for electrons to flow — its **resistance increases**. A strain gauge does exactly this, but with a tiny metallic foil bonded to a structure. When the structure bends or stretches by a microscopic amount, the foil stretches with it, and its resistance changes. This tiny change in resistance is precisely measured to calculate how much the structure deformed.

## 1.2 Strain Gauge Load Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **load cell** is a force transducer that converts an applied mechanical force (load, weight, tension, or compression) into a measurable electrical signal, using **multiple strain gauges arranged in a Wheatstone bridge configuration** on a precisely machined elastic element (spring member).

- **Standard Output:** Typically **2 mV/V** at full scale (rated output).
- **Common Types:** S-type, Canister, Beam-type, Pancake, Single-point.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of a bathroom weighing scale. When you step on it, a metal block inside slightly bends. Tiny strain gauges glued to that metal block detect this microscopic bending in **four directions** (two stretched, two compressed). The difference in their resistances is converted to a voltage that the digital display reads as your weight. That's a load cell — it converts force into electricity.

## 1.3 Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Resistance Thermometer (RTD)** is a temperature sensor that exploits the predictable change in electrical resistance of a pure metal (most commonly **Platinum (Pt)**) with temperature. The relationship is approximately linear, with resistance **increasing** as temperature increases.

- **Standard Reference:** Platinum RTD with **R₀ = 100 Ω at 0°C** (known as **Pt100**).
- **Temperature Range:** **−200°C to +850°C** (for Platinum).
- **Standard:** IEC 60751 (formerly IEC 751) defines the Callendar-Van Dusen equation for RTDs.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a long wire through which you pass current. If you heat the wire, the atoms vibrate more vigorously, making it harder for electrons to pass — **resistance increases**. For metals, this increase is very predictable and nearly linear with temperature. An RTD exploits this: by precisely measuring the resistance, we know the temperature.

## 1.4 Thermistor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Thermistor** (Thermal Resistor) is a semiconductor-based temperature sensor whose resistance changes **non-linearly** and **dramatically** with temperature. The two main types are:
> - **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **decreases** as temperature rises (most common).
> - **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **increases** as temperature rises (used in resettable fuses).

- **Material:** Sintered metal oxides (e.g., **manganese, cobalt, nickel oxides**).
- **Sensitivity:** ~10× more sensitive than RTDs (typically **−3% to −6% per °C**).
- **Range:** **−55°C to +150°C** (limited compared to RTD).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Picture a sponge. A dry sponge (cold thermistor) is hard to push water through — high resistance. As you soak the sponge in warm water (heat it), it swells and becomes very porous — water (current) flows easily — low resistance. A thermistor behaves the opposite way of an RTD: it loses resistance as it gets hotter, but it does so **much more dramatically** (non-linearly) over a smaller temperature range.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strain Gauge — Operational Principle

A strain gauge works on three fundamental effects when stretched:
1. **Length increases** (L → L + dL) → resistance increases
2. **Cross-sectional area decreases** (A → A − dA) → resistance increases
3. **Resistivity changes** (ρ → ρ + dρ) due to piezoresistive effect → resistance changes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between these three effects is the foundation of the **Gauge Factor derivation** (covered exhaustively in Section 3).

## 2.2 Load Cell — Bridge Configuration

A typical load cell uses a **4-arm Wheatstone bridge** with 4 strain gauges:
- **2 gauges in tension** (R + dR)
- **2 gauges in compression** (R − dR)

This **full-bridge configuration** provides:
- Maximum sensitivity (4× single gauge)
- Automatic temperature compensation (common-mode rejection)
- Linearity preservation

## 2.3 Resistance Thermometer — Lead Wire Configurations

The challenge with RTDs is that the **lead wires** themselves have resistance, and this resistance adds in series with the RTD, causing measurement error, especially for low-resistance sensors (like Pt100).

| Configuration | Lead Wires | Error Compensation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2-Wire** | 2 leads | None — full lead resistance adds to RTD | Short distances, high-resistance RTDs (Pt1000) |
| **3-Wire** | 3 leads | Partial — uses Wheatstone bridge to cancel lead resistance | Most industrial applications |
| **4-Wire (Kelvin)** | 4 leads | Full — separates current & voltage paths | High-precision lab & calibration |

## 2.4 Thermistor — Mathematical Models

Two common equations:
- **B-parameter equation (most common for NTC):**
  R_T = R_0 · exp[B · (1/T − 1/T_0)]
- **Steinhart-Hart equation (high accuracy):**
  1/T = a + b·ln(R) + c·(ln(R))³

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **exam-ready formula summary** — memorize these for KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistance of conductor | R = ρL/A | ρ: resistivity, L: length, A: area | Ω, Ω·m, m, m² |
| 2 | Strain | ε = ΔL/L | ΔL: change in length | dimensionless (m/m) |
| 3 | Gauge Factor | G_F = (ΔR/R)/ε | ΔR: change in resistance | dimensionless |
| 4 | Gauge Factor (full) | G_F = 1 + 2ν + (Δρ/ρ)/ε | ν: Poisson's ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | For metallic gauges | G_F ≈ 1 + 2ν ≈ 2.0 | (for ν ≈ 0.5) | dimensionless |
| 6 | RTD linear approx | R_T = R_0(1 + α·ΔT) | α: temp. coeff. (Pt: 3.85×10⁻³/°C) | Ω, °C⁻¹ |
| 7 | RTD Callendar-Van Dusen | R_T = R_0[1 + αT + βT² + γ(T−100)T³] | β, γ: coefficients | Ω |
| 8 | Thermistor (NTC) | R_T = R_0·exp[B(1/T − 1/T_0)] | B: β constant (3000–5000 K), T in Kelvin | Ω, K |
| 9 | Thermistor sensitivity | α_T = −B/T² | at temperature T | K⁻¹ |
| 10 | Load cell full-bridge output | V_o = V_ex · G_F · ε · (1+ν) | V_ex: excitation voltage | V |
| 11 | Load cell ¼-bridge output | V_o = (V_ex/4)·G_F·ε | single active gauge | V |
| 12 | 2-wire RTD error | ΔT_error = (2·R_lead)/(R_0·α) | R_lead: lead resistance | °C |
| 13 | 3-wire RTD error | ΔT_error = R_lead/(R_0·α) | (one lead in adjacent arm) | °C |
| 14 | 4-wire RTD error | ΔT_error ≈ 0 | (theoretical) | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder for KTU Answers:**
> - **Strain ε** is always dimensionless but usually given in **µε (microstrain)**.
> - **Temperature** in thermistor equation **must be in Kelvin (K)**.
> - **Gauge factor G_F** is always **dimensionless** (typically 1.8 to 2.2 for metallic gauges).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Strain Gauge Gauge Factor (G_F)

This is the **most important derivation** in this module for KTU ESE — expect it as a 7-mark question.

### Step 1: Start with the fundamental resistance equation

The electrical resistance of a uniform conductor is given by:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Where:
- **R** = electrical resistance (Ω)
- **ρ** = resistivity of the material (Ω·m)
- **L** = length of the conductor (m)
- **A** = cross-sectional area (m²)

### Step 2: Take the differential of the equation

When the conductor is subjected to strain, all three quantities (ρ, L, A) change. Taking the logarithmic differential:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** We use logarithmic differentiation because R is a product/quotient of variables. The rule is: d(x·y) = xy(dx/x + dy/dy) and d(x/y) = x/y(dx/x − dy/y). Applying this term-by-term:
> - d(ρ) → dρ/ρ
> - d(L) → dL/L
> - d(1/A) → −dA/A
> Summing gives the expression above.

### Step 3: Express the area change using Poisson's ratio

For a cylindrical conductor with circular cross-section A = πr²:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr \implies \frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r}$$

By definition of **Poisson's ratio (ν)**:

$$\nu = -\frac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{longitudinal strain}} = -\frac{dr/r}{dL/L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot (-\nu \cdot \varepsilon) = -2\nu\varepsilon$$

### Step 4: Substitute and group terms

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - (-2\nu\varepsilon)$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

### Step 5: Define Gauge Factor

The **Gauge Factor (G_F)** is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to strain:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\cdot\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of each term:**
> - **"1"** → contribution from change in **length** (geometric effect)
> - **"2ν"** → contribution from change in **area** (geometric effect)
> - **"(Δρ/ρ)/ε"** → contribution from change in **resistivity** (piezoresistive effect)
>
> For **metallic strain gauges** (e.g., Constantan), the piezoresistive term is **negligible**, so:
> $$G_F \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0$$
>
> For **semiconductor strain gauges**, the piezoresistive term **dominates**, and G_F can be **50 to 200**.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: 2-Wire RTD Lead Resistance Error

### Step 1: Total measured resistance

In 2-wire configuration, the lead wires are in series with the RTD:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2R_{lead}$$

### Step 2: Apparent temperature error

Using the linear RTD equation R_T = R_0(1 + α·T), the error in temperature is:

$$\Delta T_{error} = \frac{R_{measured} - R_{RTD}}{R_0 \cdot \alpha} = \frac{2R_{lead}}{R_0 \cdot \alpha}$$

**Numerical Example (for KTU practice):**
- Pt100 RTD: R_0 = 100 Ω, α = 3.85 × 10⁻³ /°C
- Lead wire: 10 m of copper, ~0.7 Ω each → R_lead ≈ 0.7 Ω (one way)

$$\Delta T_{error} = \frac{2 \times 0.7}{100 \times 3.85 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.4}{0.385} \approx 3.64°C$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **HUGE error** — a 3.6°C error over a 10 m cable run is unacceptable for most industrial applications. This is precisely why 2-wire RTDs are limited to short-distance or Pt1000 (1000 Ω) applications.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: 3-Wire RTD Error Cancellation (Wheatstone Bridge)

### Step 1: Standard 3-wire configuration in Wheatstone bridge

A 3-wire RTD is connected into a Wheatstone bridge with:
- **R1** = RTD element (R_T + R_lead1)
- **R2** = fixed resistor (R + R_lead1) ← same lead as R1
- **R3, R4** = precision fixed resistors (R)

### Step 2: Bridge balance condition

At balance, V_out = 0:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4} \implies \frac{R_T + R_{lead1}}{R + R_{lead1}} = \frac{R}{R}$$

This gives a balance that depends only on R_T (since both R1 and R2 have R_lead1). The lead resistance **cancels out** if the bridge is balanced.

### Step 3: Off-balance (actual measurement)

When temperature changes, the bridge is slightly off-balance. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

The lead resistance affects both adjacent arms equally, so the **bridge remains compensated**.

> [!NOTE]
> The error reduces to:
> $$\Delta T_{error} \approx \frac{R_{lead}}{R_0 \cdot \alpha} \text{ (one lead contribution instead of two)}$$
> For the same example: ΔT_error ≈ 0.7/(100 × 3.85×10⁻³) ≈ **1.82°C** — better, but not perfect.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: 4-Wire (Kelvin) RTD — True Zero Error

### Step 1: Separate current and voltage paths

In a 4-wire RTD:
- **2 wires (I+, I−)** carry the excitation current through the RTD
- **2 wires (V+, V−)** measure the voltage directly across the RTD element using a **high-impedance voltmeter**

### Step 2: Effect of high-impedance voltmeter

Since the voltmeter draws **negligible current** (I_V ≈ 0), there is **no voltage drop** across the voltage sense leads:

$$V_{measured} = I_{ex} \cdot R_{RTD} \quad \text{(exactly)}$$

The lead resistance in the voltage sense wires contributes **zero error**.

### Step 3: Why the current leads don't matter either

Although the current leads have resistance, the high-impedance voltmeter measures the **true potential difference** across the RTD element only. Hence:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{error} = 0 \text{ (theoretically)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4-wire (Kelvin) configuration** is the gold standard for **laboratory and calibration-grade** temperature measurements. It eliminates lead wire error completely.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Load Cell Full-Bridge Output

### Step 1: Wheatstone bridge output for small ΔR

For a balanced bridge with all arms R, if R1 → R + ΔR and R3 → R − ΔR, the differential output is:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R - \Delta R}{2R} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

### Step 2: Convert ΔR/R to strain

From the gauge factor: ΔR/R = G_F · ε

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 3: Account for Poisson effect in load cell

In a load cell, the axial strain (ε_axial) and transverse strain (ε_transverse) are related by Poisson's ratio:
ε_transverse = −ν · ε_axial

The transverse gauges contribute:
- Tension gauges (2 in axial): ΔR/R = +G_F·ε
- Compression gauges (2 in transverse): ΔR/R = +G_F·ν·ε (compression reduces R, but bridge inverts sign)

Net differential:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (1 + \nu)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why load cell outputs are quoted as **"2 mV/V"** — it accounts for the (1+ν) factor. For ν = 0.3, the multiplier is 1.3, and a typical G_F = 2.0 gives 2.0 × 1.3 = 2.6 mV/V per unit strain, rounded to ~2 mV/V in datasheets.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Parameter | Strain Gauge | Load Cell (Full Bridge) | RTD (Pt100) | Thermistor (NTC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output** | ΔR (resistance change) | Voltage (mV/V) | Resistance (Ω) | Resistance (Ω) |
| **Sensitivity** | G_F ≈ 2 (metal) | 1–3 mV/V | ~0.385 Ω/°C | ~−4%/°C |
| **Linearity** | Linear | Linear | Nearly linear | Highly non-linear |
| **Range** | ±3000 µε | 1 kg – 1000 t | −200 to 850°C | −55 to 150°C |
| **Self-heating** | Low | Low | Requires care | **High** (must limit current) |
| **Lead wire issue** | Half/Full bridge solves it | Built-in | **Major concern (2/3/4 wire)** | Usually short leads |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Medium-High | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
