# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Explain the working principle of a Pyroelectric Infrared (PIR) sensor. State two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A PIR sensor consists of a **ferroelectric crystal** (typically Lithium Tantalate, LiTaO₃) with two electrodes. When incident IR radiation falls on the crystal, it heats up slightly, and the **change in spontaneous polarization** generates a surface charge:

$$Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

The output current is:

$$I_p = p \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$

A **Fresnel lens** focuses IR radiation from moving warm bodies (humans at 310 K) onto the crystal, producing a pulsed signal as the body moves across different lens segments. A **JFET source follower** integrated into the sensor package provides a low-impedance output.

**Applications:**
1. Automatic lighting in corridors and staircases.
2. Intrusion / burglar alarm systems.

*(3 marks: 1 for crystal mechanism, 1 for equation / formula, 1 for two correct applications.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare ultrasonic proximity sensors and microwave level switches based on wave type, working medium, and dust immunity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic Proximity | Microwave Level Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **Wave type** | Acoustic (mechanical) wave | Electromagnetic wave |
| **Working medium / propagation** | Requires a medium (air, gas) | Travels through vacuum, air, and most non-conductors |
| **Operating frequency** | 40 kHz typical | 10 GHz to 77 GHz |
| **Dust / foam immunity** | Poor (dust scatters and absorbs acoustic energy) | Excellent (microwaves pass through dust and foam) |
| **Speed** | $v_s \approx 343$ m/s (slow) | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (fast) |
| **Typical range** | cm to 10 m | 0.1 m to 100 m |

**Inference:** Microwave switches are preferred in **dusty industrial environments** (cement, flour, plastic pellets), whereas ultrasonic sensors are **cheaper and adequate for clean applications** (water tanks, car reversing).

*(3 marks: 1 for wave-type distinction, 1 for medium/propagation, 1 for dust immunity comparison with inference.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory — solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**With neat block diagrams, explain the construction and working of:**
**(a) An Optical Level Sensor using total internal reflection.** *(7 marks)*
**(b) An Ultrasonic Level Sensor used in a chemical storage tank.** *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Construction (2 marks):**
An optical level sensor consists of a **transparent polymer or glass prism** (typically acrylic with $n_1 \approx 1.49$) shaped as a **triangular tip** at the end of a probe. Inside the housing, an **LED (emitter)** directs light into the prism, and a **phototransistor (receiver)** is mounted to capture the internally reflected light. The output is conditioned by a **transimpedance amplifier** followed by a **Schmitt trigger** that produces a clean digital output.

**2. Working Principle (3 marks):**
The sensor is mounted on the side of a tank such that the prism tip protrudes inward. When the tip is in **air** (refractive index $n_2 = 1.00$), the light striking the inner surface of the prism undergoes **total internal reflection (TIR)** because the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.00}{1.49}\right) = 42.2°$$

The reflected light strikes the phototransistor, which produces a **HIGH output** — the sensor reads **"DRY"**.

When the liquid level rises and **wets the prism tip** (water, $n_2 = 1.33$), the refractive-index contrast changes. The new critical angle becomes:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.33}{1.49}\right) = 63.1°$$

The original angle of incidence (≈45°) is now **less than the new critical angle**, so TIR is destroyed — light **refracts out** into the liquid. The phototransistor receives almost no light, producing a **LOW output** — the sensor reads **"WET"**.

**3. Applications (1 mark):**
Used in pharmaceutical, food & beverage, and medical IV-line liquid detection; leak detection in pipelines.

**4. Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
LED → Prism Tip → TIR or Refraction → Phototransistor → Amplifier → Schmitt Trigger → Digital Output
```

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Construction & Mounting (2 marks):**
An ultrasonic level sensor comprises a **piezoelectric transducer** (PZT ceramic disc with conical horn), housed in a **weatherproof IP65/IP67 enclosure**, mounted at the **top of the tank** looking vertically downward. A **microcontroller** generates the transmit pulse, measures the echo time, applies temperature compensation, and outputs a **4–20 mA** or **HART** signal proportional to the liquid level.

**2. Working Principle (3 marks):**
The microcontroller triggers a **40 kHz burst** of typically **8 to 16 cycles**. The transducer launches this pulse into the air column above the liquid. The pulse travels down, hits the **liquid surface**, and reflects back. The same transducer receives the echo. The **round-trip time** $t_{ToF}$ is measured.

The level $L$ is calculated as:

$$L = H - \frac{v_s \cdot t_{ToF}}{2}$$

where $H$ is the sensor-to-bottom distance (tank height) and $v_s$ is temperature-corrected using:

$$v_s = 331.4 + 0.6T \;\; \text{(m/s, T in °C)}$$

**3. Key Considerations (1 mark):**
- **Blanking zone / dead band:** 20–50 cm — the sensor cannot measure within this range.
- **Foam suppression:** Foam absorbs acoustic energy; FMCW radar or guided-wave radar is preferred for foamy liquids.

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- No contact with corrosive / hazardous liquid.
- Works for any liquid regardless of conductivity, transparency, or chemical nature.
- One sensor handles entire range of tank height.

**5. Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Microcontroller → 40 kHz Burst → Piezo Transmitter → Air → Liquid Surface → Echo → Piezo Receiver → Amplifier → ToF Counter → Level L = H − v·t/2 → 4–20 mA Output
```

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Explain the following with principle, governing equations, block diagram and two applications each:**
**(a) Infrared (IR) proximity sensor.** *(7 marks)*
**(b) Microwave level switch using FMCW principle.** *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
An IR proximity sensor operates on **two complementary mechanisms**:
- *Active:* An IR LED (typically **940 nm GaAs**) emits a beam that reflects off the target and is detected by a photodiode.
- *Passive (thermal):* A pyroelectric detector senses the IR radiation naturally emitted by warm objects per the **Stefan–Boltzmann law**:

$$P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$$

**2. Governing Equation (2 marks):**
For the active triangulation mode, range is:

$$x = \frac{f \cdot d}{p}$$

where $f$ = focal length, $d$ = spot displacement on PSD, $p$ = emitter-receiver baseline.

For thermal detection, the **pyroelectric current** is:

$$I_p = p \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
DC Source → 40 kHz Oscillator → IR LED → Target → Reflected Beam → IR Photodiode → Bandpass Filter → Amplifier → Comparator → Output
```

**4. Applications (2 marks):**
1. Automatic sliding doors in malls and hospitals.
2. Non-contact infrared forehead thermometers.
3. Object counting on conveyor belts.
4. Burglar alarm systems.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
A microwave level switch is a **point-level detector** that uses a **Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW)** at microwave frequencies (typically **24 GHz**) emitted by a **Gunn diode oscillator**. The wave reflects off the process material and is mixed with a sample of the transmitted signal. The mixer output yields an **intermediate frequency (IF)** proportional to the distance.

**2. Governing Equation (2 marks):**
The IF frequency is:

$$f_{IF} = \frac{2 R \cdot \Delta f}{c \cdot T_c}$$

where $R$ = range to material surface, $\Delta f$ = sweep deviation (e.g., 200 MHz), $T_c$ = sweep period (e.g., 1 ms), and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

For a Doppler system:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$$

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
VCO → Gunn Oscillator 24 GHz → Antenna → Material → Reflected → Mixer → IF Amplifier → Detector → Comparator → Relay Output
```

**4. Applications (2 marks):**
1. Cement silos and fly-ash hoppers (dusty environments).
2. Molten metal level detection in foundries.
3. Point-level detection in oil refinery distillation columns.
4. Plastic pellet and grain silo monitoring.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE for This Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = v \cdot t / 2$. Examiners will deduct 1 to 2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing units of temperature:** Use Kelvin in Stefan–Boltzmann, Celsius in the $v_s = 331.4 + 0.6T$ formula. Mixing them up leads to a wrong answer.
> 3. **Omitting the temperature compensation discussion** in ultrasonic answers. The examiner's key specifically allocates 1 mark to it.
> 4. **Drawing a sensor and not labeling the energy domain** (EM / acoustic). A bare block diagram without labels gets only partial credit.
> 5. **Writing "PIR" without expanding** "Pyroelectric Infrared" on first use. KTU deducts 0.5 mark for unexpanded acronyms.
> 6. **Stating "microwave level sensor measures level" without explaining FMCW or Doppler** — this is the most common incomplete answer. Always state *how* the range is extracted from the reflected wave.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Non-contact transducers** never touch the target — they exploit a wave field.
- The **universal ToF equation** is $R = v \cdot t / 2$; the factor of 2 accounts for the round trip.
- **Infrared sensors** rely on **thermal radiation** (Stefan–Boltzmann) or **active LED + photodiode** triangulation.
- **PIR sensors** are AC-coupled — they detect *change* in IR, not steady IR. They are blind to stationary bodies.
- **Ultrasonic sensors** use **40 kHz acoustic pulses** in air; speed of sound = 343 m/s at 20°C, varies as $v_s = 331.4 + 0.6T$.
- **Ultrasonic sensors have a dead zone (blanking distance)** of 20–50 cm directly below the transducer.
- **Microwave level switches** use **FMCW or Doppler**; $f_{IF} = 2R\Delta f / (cT_c)$ for FMCW, $f_d = 2vf_0\cos\theta / c$ for Doppler.
- **Microwaves penetrate dust, vapor, and foam** — they are the sensor of choice in harsh process industries.
- **Optical level sensors** use **total internal reflection (TIR)** in a prism; TIR is destroyed when the prism touches a liquid, producing a digital "wet/dry" output.
- **Critical angle** for TIR is $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- **Ultrasonic level sensor** measures $L = H - v_s t_{ToF} / 2$ — the only difference from a proximity sensor is the **subtraction of tank height** $H$.
- **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and **speed of sound** $v_s \approx 343$ m/s — a factor of **~10⁶** difference — this is why IR/optical sensors have **virtually no measurable ToF delay** at short distances (a 3 m round trip takes 20 ns, demanding GHz-band electronics), while ultrasonic ToF is easily measured with a microcontroller (3 m round trip takes 17.5 ms).
- **Three industrial "go-to" choices:**
    - Clean, short-range, no dust → **IR or Optical**
    - Liquid level in a moderate tank → **Ultrasonic**
    - Dusty, hot, or foamy process → **Microwave**
- **The dielectric constant of water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$)** is why microwave sensors work brilliantly on water-based liquids even when optics and acoustics fail.
- **A human body emits ~90 W of IR power** — this is the engineering basis of every thermal camera and PIR motion detector.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyrometers: Optical, Radiation pyrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEIT304 - SENSORS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pyrometers: Optical, Radiation pyrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pyrometer?

A **Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument specifically designed to measure very high temperatures (typically above **600 °C**, where conventional thermometers like thermocouples, RTDs, or liquid-in-glass thermometers fail due to physical limitations, material degradation, or contamination). The word is derived from the Greek *pyr* (fire) and *metron* (measure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pyrometers are radiation thermometers classified under **non-contact temperature measurement** techniques. They exploit the **thermal radiation laws** (Planck's, Wien's, Stefan-Boltzmann) emitted by every hot body above absolute zero.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants Covered in This Module

| Type | Principle | Wavelength Used | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical Pyrometer** | Monochromatic (single-wavelength) brightness comparison | A narrow band (often **0.65 μm red**) | **600 °C – 3000 °C** |
| **Radiation Pyrometer** | Total (broadband) thermal radiation measurement | All wavelengths | **600 °C – 4000 °C** |

---

### 1.3 Optical Pyrometer — Definition

An **Optical Pyrometer** (specifically the *disappearing-filament type*) is a device that measures the temperature of a glowing hot body by **visually matching the brightness of a calibrated internal tungsten filament against the brightness of the target body** when both are viewed through a red filter at a specific reference wavelength (≈ **0.65 μm**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *An optical pyrometer is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument that uses the principle of comparison of monochromatic brightness (luminance) of the hot body with that of a reference filament lamp, the comparison being made at a specific wavelength using a red filter, thereby allowing the operator to determine the target temperature from a calibrated current-vs-temperature scale.*

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy for Optical Pyrometer

> **Intuition:** Imagine you are standing in a room with two lamps — one is the "target" furnace glowing in the distance, and the other is a small reference lamp whose brightness you can control with a dimmer. By adjusting your dimmer knob, you make the small lamp look *exactly as bright* as the distant furnace (the filament "disappears" against the furnace background). The dimmer-knob position directly tells you how hot the furnace is. This is exactly how a disappearing-filament optical pyrometer works.

---

### 1.5 Radiation Pyrometer — Definition

A **Radiation Pyrometer** (also called a *total-radiation pyrometer* or *radiation thermometer*) is a non-contact instrument that measures temperature by collecting the **total thermal radiant energy emitted by the hot body** over a wide wavelength spectrum, and focusing this energy onto a thermal detector (typically a **thermopile**) whose output EMF is proportional to the **fourth power of absolute temperature** of the target (Stefan-Boltzmann Law).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A radiation pyrometer is a non-contact temperature sensor that absorbs the total radiant flux emitted by a hot target over a broad spectral range, converts it into a measurable electrical signal (EMF) using a thermopile or similar thermal detector, and infers the target temperature via the Stefan-Boltzmann fourth-power law.*

### 1.6 Real-World Analogy for Radiation Pyrometer

> **Intuition:** Think of sitting near a campfire. Your skin feels warm not because of the hot air, but because of the **invisible and visible electromagnetic radiation** (infrared + light) the fire emits. The hotter the fire, the more energy radiates out, and the more warmth you feel — and the relationship is not linear; it follows a *fourth-power* rule. A radiation pyrometer is essentially a calibrated version of your skin: it "feels" the fire's radiation, converts it to a small voltage, and translates that voltage to temperature.

---

### 1.7 Why Use Pyrometers Instead of Contact Sensors?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **High Temperatures:** Thermocouples (Type R/S/B) melt or degrade above ~1700 °C; pyrometers work up to 4000 °C.
> 2. **Moving Targets:** Measures temperature of molten metal, rotating kiln walls, turbine blades without contact.
> 3. **Hazardous / Reactive Environments:** No probe contamination, no chemical reaction with the target.
> 4. **Fast Response:** Microsecond-level response since only radiation transfer is involved (no conduction/convection delay).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Physics Behind Pyrometers

Every body above absolute zero (**0 K = −273.15 °C**) emits electromagnetic radiation. The intensity and spectral distribution of this radiation depend on the **absolute temperature** and **emissivity** of the body. Pyrometers exploit this universal physical law.

---

### 2.2 Optical Pyrometer — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. The hot target body emits radiation across the spectrum, including visible light, with a peak governed by **Wien's Displacement Law**.
2. A **red optical filter** (typically **λ ≈ 0.65 μm**) restricts the radiation to a narrow monochromatic band, eliminating color-distortion errors.
3. The **objective lens** forms an image of the target in the plane of a calibrated **tungsten reference filament**.
4. The **eyepiece** allows the operator to view both the filament and the target image simultaneously.
5. A **rheostat** controls the current through the filament. The current controls the filament temperature, which controls its **monochromatic brightness**.
6. The operator adjusts the rheostat until the filament **"disappears"** — i.e., its brightness is identical to the target's brightness at the reference wavelength.
7. The **galvanometer current** at the moment of disappearance is read off a calibrated scale, giving the target temperature directly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a red filter?** Human eye sensitivity to brightness is most stable in the red region, and most industrial flames/glowing bodies emit sufficient red light even at lower temperatures. Using red also makes the brightness comparison mono-chromatic, which is critical because Wien's law-based single-wavelength analysis requires a single λ.

### 2.3 Optical Pyrometer — Constructional Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Objective Lens** | Focuses the target's image onto the filament plane |
| **Tungsten Filament** | Reference brightness source; calibrated against black body |
| **Rheostat (Variable Resistor)** | Adjusts filament current and hence brightness |
| **Red Filter (λ ≈ 0.65 μm)** | Restricts comparison to single wavelength |
| **Eyepiece** | Magnifies and allows the operator to view filament + image |
| **Galvanometer / Ammeter** | Indicates filament current; calibrated in °C |
| **Battery** | Powers the filament circuit |

### 2.4 Optical Pyrometer — Calibration

The instrument is calibrated by sighting it on a **blackbody furnace** of known temperature. The filament current at which the filament disappears is recorded for several calibration temperatures. A plot of current (or resistance) vs. temperature is the calibration curve.

> [!WARNING]
> For non-blackbody (real) targets, the pyrometer reads *brightness temperature* $T_b$, not the true temperature $T$. The true temperature is recovered using: $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}\right)$, where $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K and $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is the spectral emissivity.

---

### 2.5 Radiation Pyrometer — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. The hot target emits **total thermal radiation** (all wavelengths), governed by **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $E = \sigma T^4$.
2. An **objective lens (or concave mirror)** collects a portion of this radiation and focuses it onto a **thermal detector** (typically a **thermopile** — multiple series-connected thermocouples).
3. The detector absorbs the radiation, its hot-junction temperature rises, and a **DC EMF** is generated.
4. This EMF is measured by a sensitive millivoltmeter calibrated directly in **°C**.
5. The cold-junction of the thermopile is usually shielded, so we approximate cold-junction temperature as $T_0 \approx$ ambient.

### 2.6 Radiation Pyrometer — Constructional Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Objective Lens (or Mirror)** | Collects and focuses radiation onto detector |
| **Aperture / Diaphragm** | Defines solid angle of radiation collected |
| **Thermopile (Blackened Hot Junctions)** | Converts radiant energy to EMF |
| **Cold Junctions** | Maintained at known temperature (often ambient) |
| **Eyepiece / Sighting System** | Operator aims the instrument at the target |
| **D.C. Millivoltmeter** | Displays the EMF; scale calibrated in °C |
| **Compensating Circuit** | For ambient temperature drift of cold junction |

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic. Memorize these equations and their boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Constants | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Blackbody)** | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | $E_b$ = total emissive power (W/m²); $T$ = absolute temperature (K) |
| 2 | **Stefan-Boltzmann (Real Body)** | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1) | For non-blackbody, multiply by ε |
| 3 | **Net Radiation Exchange** | $E_{net} = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | $T_1$ = target, $T_2$ = detector/cold body | K (absolute) |
| 4 | **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2898$ | $\lambda_{max}$ in μm, $T$ in K | Peak wavelength inversely proportional to T |
| 5 | **Planck's Spectral Distribution** | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1 \lambda^{-5}}{e^{C_2/\lambda T}-1}$ | $C_1 = 3.74 \times 10^{-16}$ W·m²; $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K | Full form; valid for all λ, T |
| 6 | **Wien's Approximation** (λT ≪ C₂) | $E_{b\lambda} \approx C_1 \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$ | Valid when $\lambda T < 3000$ μm·K | Used in optical pyrometer analysis |
| 7 | **Brightness Temperature Relation** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}\right)$ | $T_b$ = brightness temp; $T$ = true temp | Applies to optical pyrometer |
| 8 | **Thermopile EMF Output (Radiation Pyrometer)** | $e = K_a A \sigma \left(T_t^4 - T_d^4\right)$ | $K_a$ = absorption factor, $A$ = area, $T_t$ = target, $T_d$ = detector | $e$ in volts |
| 9 | **Lens Equation (Geometry)** | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ | $f$ = focal length, $u$ = object distance, $v$ = image distance | Used for objective lens design |
| 10 | **Radiant Power Collected** | $P = \sigma T^4 \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \Omega$ | $A_{lens}$ = lens area, $\Omega$ = solid angle (sr) | Determines detector signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Reminder:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) — **always use this KTU-accepted value**. $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K (second radiation constant).

### 2.8 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Optical Pyrometer (Wien's)**: Used in steel plants, foundries, and incandescent-lamp manufacturing where visible glow provides a clean monochromatic signal.
- **Radiation Pyrometer (Stefan-Boltzmann)**: Used in kiln monitoring, glass furnaces, induction heating, and aerospace exhaust temperature profiling. The fourth-power dependence means even small temperature changes produce large EMF changes → **excellent sensitivity** at high temperatures.
- **Emissivity correction** is critical in both — for non-blackbodies, neglecting ε can lead to errors exceeding **20–40 %** in true temperature.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brightness Comparison Equation for Optical Pyrometer

The optical pyrometer compares monochromatic emissive powers at wavelength $\lambda$. Using **Wien's approximation** (valid for $\lambda T \ll C_2$, i.e., visible range and moderate T):

$$
E_{b\lambda} \approx C_1 \lambda^{-5} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right)
$$

Let the target body (with emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ at the reference wavelength) emit radiation with intensity $E_{\lambda,t}$, and the reference filament emit $E_{\lambda,f}$ at the matched condition.

For the filament, $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$ (assumed blackbody at its temperature $T_f$).

**Condition for visual match (filament disappears):**

$$
\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot C_1 \lambda^{-5} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) = C_1 \lambda^{-5} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T_f}\right)
$$

Canceling $C_1 \lambda^{-5}$ from both sides:

$$
\varepsilon_\lambda \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T_f}\right)
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln \varepsilon_\lambda - \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T} = -\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T_f}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_f} - \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2}\ln \varepsilon_\lambda = \dfrac{1}{T_f} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}\right)
$$

Since the filament is calibrated against a blackbody, $T_f$ when matched to a blackbody target *is* the brightness temperature $T_b$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}\right)\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental correction equation** for converting the optical pyrometer reading (brightness temperature) into the true temperature of a non-blackbody target.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: EMF Equation for Radiation Pyrometer

Consider a thermopile-based radiation pyrometer with an objective lens of area $A$ that subtends a solid angle $\Omega$ at the target. The lens focuses the radiation onto the blackened hot junction of the thermopile.

**Step 1:** Total radiant power emitted by target per unit area (Stefan-Boltzmann):

$$
E = \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4
$$

**Step 2:** Power intercepted by the lens (aperture):

$$
P_{inc} = E \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Power absorbed by the thermopile hot junction (after passing through lens, with absorption factor $K_a$ and lens transmission $\tau$):

$$
P_{abs} = K_a \, \tau \, \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4 \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \Omega
$$

**Step 4:** The thermopile has $n$ thermocouples in series, each producing Seebeck EMF proportional to the temperature rise of the hot junction. The EMF output is:

$$
e = n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_h - T_c)
$$

**Step 5:** At steady state, the absorbed power equals the heat dissipated (radiation + conduction) from the hot junction. The hot junction temperature stabilizes such that:

$$
P_{abs} = h \cdot A_{j} \cdot (T_h - T_0)
$$

where $h$ is the effective heat-loss coefficient and $A_j$ is the junction area. For a thermopile, the relationship between EMF and incident radiant power is linear:

$$
e = S \cdot P_{abs} = S \cdot K_a \cdot \tau \cdot \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4 \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \Omega
$$

**Step 6:** Combining constants into a single instrument constant $K$:

$$
\boxed{\;e = K \cdot \sigma \left(T_t^4 - T_0^4\right)\;}
$$

where $T_0$ is the cold-junction (or detector housing) temperature, and $K$ incorporates emissivity correction and instrument geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fourth-power dependence on $T_t$ means that if temperature *doubles* (in K), the EMF increases by a factor of **16**. This is the source of the radiation pyrometer's high sensitivity at elevated temperatures.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Optical vs. Radiation Pyrometer

| Parameter | Optical Pyrometer | Radiation Pyrometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Law** | Wien's / Planck's (monochromatic) | Stefan-Boltzmann (total) |
| **Wavelength Selection** | Narrow band, λ ≈ 0.65 μm (red filter) | All wavelengths (broadband) |
| **Detector** | Human eye (visual comparison) | Thermopile (EMF measurement) |
| **Subjectivity** | Subjective (operator-dependent) | Objective (electrical readout) |
| **Output** | Manual scale reading | Continuous electrical signal |
| **Emissivity Error** | Correction requires λ & ελ | Correction requires total ε |
| **Range** | 600 °C – 3000 °C | 600 °C – 4000 °C |
| **Continuous Monitoring** | No (manual sighting) | Yes (fixed installation possible) |
| **Calibration** | Filament current vs. blackbody temp | Thermopile EMF vs. blackbody temp |
| **Atmospheric Absorption** | Less affected (visible band) | More affected (H₂O, CO₂ absorb IR) |
| **Response Time** | Slow (operator decision) | Fast (thermopile time constant ~ 1 s) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Moderate to high |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Emissivity Correction (Optical Pyrometer)

> **Problem:** An optical pyrometer with a red filter of λ = 0.65 μm is sighted on a target whose spectral emissivity at 0.65 μm is $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.6$. The pyrometer reads a brightness temperature of $T_b = 1473$ K (1200 °C). Calculate the true temperature $T$ of the target. Take $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K.

**Solution:**

Using the brightness-temperature correction equation:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}\right)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1473} + \dfrac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{0.6}\right)
$$

Computing each term:

- Term 1: $1/1473 = 6.7889 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹
- Term 2 coefficient: $\dfrac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.518 \times 10^{-5}$ m·K / (m·K) = $4.518 \times 10^{-5}$ K⁻¹
- $\ln(1/0.6) = \ln(1.6667) = 0.5108$

Term 2 value: $4.518 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5108 = 2.308 \times 10^{-5}$ K⁻¹

Adding:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = 6.7889 \times 10^{-4} + 2.308 \times 10^{-5} = 7.0197 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{7.0197 \times 10^{-4}} = 1424.6 \text{ K}
$$

Converting to Celsius:

$$
T = 1424.6 - 273.15 = 1151.5 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The actual temperature of the target is **~48.5 °C lower** than the pyrometer reading. The pyrometer always reads *higher* than the true temperature for non-blackbodies (since less radiation is emitted than a blackbody at the same T, the instrument "thinks" the body is hotter than it is to produce the same brightness). This is a classic KTU 14-mark problem.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Radiation Pyrometer EMF

> **Problem:** A radiation pyrometer is sighted on a target at $T_t = 1800$ K. The cold-junction (detector housing) is at $T_0 = 300$ K. The instrument constant is $K = 5.5 \times 10^{-9}$ V·K⁻⁴. Find the EMF output. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

**Solution:**

$$
e = K \cdot \sigma \left(T_t^4 - T_0^4\right)
$$

Computing $T_t^4$ and $T_0^4$:

- $T_t^4 = 1800^4 = 1.04976 \times 10^{13}$ K⁴
- $T_0^4 = 300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}$ K⁴
- Difference: $T_t^4 - T_0^4 \approx 1.04976 \times 10^{13} - 0.000081 \times 10^{13} \approx 1.04895 \times 10^{13}$ K⁴ (the $T_0^4$ term is negligible at 0.00077 % — we often drop it for $T_t \gg T_0$)

$$
e = (5.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.04895 \times 10^{13})
$$

Stepwise multiplication:

- $5.5 \times 5.67 = 31.185$
- $31.185 \times 1.04895 = 32.71$
- Powers: $10^{-9} \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{13} = 10^{-4}$

$$
e = 32.71 \times 10^{-4} = 3.271 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 3.27 \text{ mV}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The EMF is in millivolts — a sensitive **potentiometric recorder** or **nanovolt amplifier** is needed. Also note that if temperature increased to 2000 K, the EMF would jump by a factor of $(2000/1800)^4 = 1.524$, i.e., to about **4.99 mV** — a 52 % increase for just an 11 % temperature rise. This **fourth-power sensitivity** is what makes radiation pyrometers excellent for high-T applications.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Optical Pyrometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["TARGET SIDE"]
        A1["Hot Body Target\nGlowing Furnace / Molten Metal"]
    end

    subgraph INSTRUMENT["OPTICAL PYROMETER BODY"]
        B1["Objective Lens\nCollects & Focuses Light"]
        B2["Red Filter\nlambda approx 0.65 micrometer"]
        B3["Tungsten Reference Filament\nCalibrated Brightness Source"]
        B4["Rheostat\nVariable Resistor"]
        B5["Battery\nDC Power Supply"]
        B6["Eyepiece\nViewing System"]
        B7["Galvanometer\nCurrent Indicator"]
        B8["Calibrated Scale\nin degree Celsius"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OPERATOR SIDE"]
        C1["Operator Eye\nVisual Match Check"]
        C2["Temperature Reading\nDisplay"]
    end

    A1 -- "Radiant Energy" --> B1
    B1 -- "Focused Image" --> B3
    B5 -- "DC Current" --> B4
    B4 -- "Controlled Current" --> B3
    B2 -- "Monochromatic Beam" --> B6
    B3 -- "Reference Brightness" --> B6
    B6 -- "Combined View" --> C1
    C1 -- "Adjust Rheostat" --> B4
    B3 -- "Filament Current" --> B7
    B7 -- "Deflection" --> B8
    B8 -- "Indicated Temperature" --> C2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The hot target emits radiation, which is collected by the objective lens and projected as an image at the plane of the tungsten filament. The operator views both the target image and the filament through the eyepiece (with the red filter ensuring monochromatic comparison). Adjusting the rheostat changes the filament current, altering its brightness until it matches the target's. The matched current is read off a calibrated temperature scale.

---

### 4.2 Radiation Pyrometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TARGET["TARGET ZONE"]
        T1["Hot Body\nT greater than 600 degree Celsius\nTotal Radiant Emitter"]
    end

    subgraph OPTICS["OPTICAL COLLECTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        O1["Objective Lens\nor Concave Mirror\nDiameter D, Focal Length f"]
        O2["Aperture Diaphragm\nDefines Solid Angle Omega"]
        O3["Eyepiece / Sighting Scope\nAiming Aid"]
    end

    subgraph DETECTION["DETECTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1["Thermopile\nn Series Thermocouples\nBlackened Hot Junctions"]
        D2["Cold Junctions\nAt Reference Temperature T0"]
        D3["Compensation Circuit\nCold Junction Compensator"]
    end

    subgraph READOUT["READOUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        R1["High-Gain DC Amplifier\nMillivolt Sensitivity"]
        R2["Digital / Analog Display\nCalibrated in degree Celsius"]
        R3["Recorder / Data Logger\nContinuous Monitoring"]
    end

    T1 -- "E = epsilon sigma T to power 4" --> O1
    O1 -- "Focused Beam" --> O2
    O2 -- "Restricted Cone" --> D1
    O3 -. "Visual Aiming" .- O1
    D1 -- "Seebeck EMF" --> R1
    D2 -- "Reference T0" --> D3
    D3 -- "Compensated Signal" --> R1
    R1 -- "Amplified mV" --> R2
    R2 -- "Display" --> R3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Total thermal radiation from the hot target passes through the optical system (lens + aperture), where it is focused onto the blackened hot junctions of a thermopile. The thermopile generates a Seebeck EMF proportional to the fourth power of target temperature (less the detector's own temperature contribution). This tiny EMF (mV level) is amplified, compensated for cold-junction drift, and displayed as a calibrated temperature reading.

---

### 4.3 Operational Decision Flow: Which Pyrometer to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Target Temperature\ngreater than 600 degree C?"}
    Q2{"Need continuous\nmonitoring?"}
    Q3{"Target has\nhigh emissivity\napprox blackbody?"}
    Q4{"Target is small\nor distant?"}

    A1["Use Thermocouple\nor RTD"]
    A2["Use RADIATION\nPYROMETER\nTotal radiation\nStefan-Boltzmann"]
    A3["Use OPTICAL\nPYROMETER\nDisappearing filament\nVisual match"]
    A4["Use OPTICAL\nwith TELESCOPE\nattachment"]
    A5["Apply emissivity\ncorrection\nor use blackbody\nenclosure"]

    Q1 -- No --> A1
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q2
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- No, not blackbody --> A5
    Q3 -- Yes, near blackbody --> Q4
    Q4 -- No --> A3
    Q4 -- Yes, small target --> A4
    A5 --> A2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** KTU exam questions often ask students to *select* the right pyrometer. Use this decision flow as a study aid: low-T → contact sensors; high-T + continuous monitoring → radiation pyrometer; high-T + spot check + visual → optical pyrometer.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an optical pyrometer. State the principle of operation of a disappearing-filament optical pyrometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **optical pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument used for measuring high temperatures, typically in the range of **600 °C to 3000 °C**, where contact-type sensors are unsuitable.

**Principle:** It works on the principle of **monochromatic brightness comparison**. The brightness of a calibrated **tungsten reference filament**, viewed through a **red filter** (λ ≈ 0.65 μm), is compared visually with the brightness of the hot target. The filament current is varied using a **rheostat** until the filament **"disappears"** against the background image of the target — i.e., both appear equally bright. The filament current at the matched condition is read on a calibrated scale, which directly indicates the target temperature.

> *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for principle. Mention of red filter and disappearing filament concept is essential for full marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a radiation pyrometer? State the physical law on which it operates and write the governing equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **radiation pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring device that determines the temperature of a hot body by detecting the **total thermal radiation** emitted by the body over a wide range of wavelengths and focusing it onto a **thermal detector** (usually a thermopile).

**Physical Law:** It operates on the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, which states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a blackbody is proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

**Governing Equation:**

$$
E_b = \sigma T^4
$$

where $E_b$ is the total emissive power (W/m²), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

> *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for law statement, 1 mark for the equation with constant.)*

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **disappearing-filament optical pyrometer**. Discuss its **advantages, limitations, and applications**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An optical pyrometer fitted with a red filter of wavelength **λ = 0.65 μm** is used to measure the temperature of a metal surface whose spectral emissivity at 0.65 μm is **ελ = 0.55**. The instrument indicates a brightness temperature of **T_b = 1500 K**. Calculate the **true temperature** of the metal surface. Take **C₂ = 1.4388 × 10⁻² m·K**. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Disappearing-Filament Optical Pyrometer — 7 marks**

**Construction (3 marks):** A typical disappearing-filament optical pyrometer consists of:
1. **Objective lens** — focuses an image of the hot target onto the plane of the reference filament.
2. **Tungsten reference filament** — a calibrated brightness source, mounted in a sealed lamp housing.
3. **Rheostat (variable resistor)** — adjusts the current flowing through the filament, controlling its temperature and hence its brightness.
4. **Battery** — DC source powering the filament circuit.
5. **Red filter (λ ≈ 0.65 μm)** — placed in the eyepiece, restricts the comparison to a single wavelength in the red region, eliminating color-matching errors and exploiting the stable response of the human eye in the red band.
6. **Eyepiece** — magnifies the combined view of target image and filament.
7. **Galvanometer / Ammeter** — displays the filament current; the scale is calibrated directly in °C.
8. **Calibration scale** — graduated in temperature units, generated by sighting the instrument on a blackbody furnace of known temperature.

**Working (3 marks):** The hot target (e.g., a furnace interior) is sighted through the eyepiece. Its image is formed at the plane of the tungsten filament. The operator views both the filament and the target image through the red filter. By adjusting the rheostat, the filament current is varied, which changes the filament temperature and its monochromatic brightness. The current is increased until the filament glows *more* brightly than the target, and decreased until it glows *less*. At a particular current, the filament brightness exactly matches that of the target at λ = 0.65 μm — the filament **"disappears"** into the background. The current at this matched condition is then read on the calibrated scale, giving the brightness temperature.

**Advantages, Limitations & Applications (1 mark):**
- **Advantages:** Simple, rugged, no power for detection (human eye is the detector), no contact, suitable for very high temperatures, good accuracy for blackbodies.
- **Limitations:** Subjective (operator-dependent), manual comparison only — not suitable for continuous recording, requires line of sight, cannot measure dull/weak targets, requires emissivity correction for non-blackbodies.
- **Applications:** Steel plants, foundries, glass manufacturing, cement kilns, incandescent lamp production, ceramic firing.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu m = 0.65 \times 10^{-6} \, m$, $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.55$, $T_b = 1500 \, K$, $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, m \cdot K$.

**To find:** True temperature $T$.

**Formula:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\ln(1/\varepsilon_\lambda) = \ln(1/0.55) = \ln(1.8182) = 0.5978$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\lambda}{C_2} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.518 \times 10^{-5} \, K^{-1}$

**Step 3:** Second term: $4.518 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5978 = 2.701 \times 10^{-5} \, K^{-1}$

**Step 4:** First term: $1/T_b = 1/1500 = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \, K^{-1}$

**Step 5:** Sum:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} + 2.701 \times 10^{-5} = 6.937 \times 10^{-4} \, K^{-1}
$$

**Step 6:** $T = 1 / 6.937 \times 10^{-4} = 1441.5 \, K$

**Step 7:** Convert to °C: $T = 1441.5 - 273.15 = 1168.3 \, °C$

**Final Answer:** The true temperature of the metal surface is **T ≈ 1441.5 K (1168.3 °C)**.

> *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark formula, 1 mark each for steps 1, 2, 3, 4; 1 mark for final answer, 1 mark for unit/correct interpretation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a radiation pyrometer**. Derive the **EMF equation** for the thermopile output. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A radiation pyrometer is used to measure the temperature of a furnace whose emissivity is **ε = 0.8**. The thermopile generates an EMF of **e = 4.2 mV** when the cold junction is at **T₀ = 300 K**, and the instrument constant is **K = 7.5 × 10⁻⁹ V·K⁻⁴**. Calculate the **true temperature of the furnace**. Take **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction, Working, and EMF Derivation of Radiation Pyrometer — 7 marks**

**Construction (2 marks):** A radiation pyrometer consists of:
1. **Objective lens (or concave mirror)** — collects radiation from the hot target.
2. **Aperture/diaphragm** — defines the solid angle of radiation collected.
3. **Thermopile** — the heart of the instrument: multiple (typically 8–16) **thermocouples connected in series**, with their hot junctions **blackened** to maximize absorption. Cold junctions are thermally anchored to the instrument housing.
4. **Cold-junction compensation circuit** — corrects for ambient temperature variations.
5. **Eyepiece or sighting scope** — for aiming at the target.
6. **DC millivoltmeter** — measures the EMF, scale calibrated directly in °C.
7. **Recorder output** — for continuous monitoring.

**Working (2 marks):** The hot target emits total thermal radiation governed by $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. The objective lens collects this radiation over its aperture and focuses it onto the blackened hot junctions of the thermopile. The hot junctions absorb the radiation, their temperature rises, and a **Seebeck EMF** is generated in proportion to the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions. For a thermopile with $n$ thermocouples, the EMF is $n$ times that of a single thermocouple. The millivoltmeter reads this EMF, and since EMF $\propto T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann), the scale can be calibrated in temperature units. The instrument is calibrated against a blackbody furnace of known temperature.

**Derivation of EMF Equation (3 marks):**

The total radiant power emitted by the target (per unit area):

$$
E = \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4
$$

Power intercepted by the objective lens of area $A$ subtending solid angle $\Omega$ at the target:

$$
P_{inc} = \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4 \cdot A \cdot \Omega
$$

Power absorbed by the thermopile (with absorption factor $K_a$ and lens transmission $\tau$):

$$
P_{abs} = K_a \tau \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4 A \Omega
$$

The EMF generated by a thermopile of sensitivity $S$ (V/W) is:

$$
e = S \cdot P_{abs} = S \cdot K_a \tau A \Omega \cdot \varepsilon \sigma T_t^4
$$

Combining all instrument constants into $K$:

$$
e = K \cdot \sigma (T_t^4 - T_0^4)
$$

where $T_0$ is the cold-junction (detector housing) temperature, and $K = S K_a \tau A \Omega \varepsilon$ incorporates the geometry and material properties.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:** $e = 4.2 \, mV = 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \, V$, $T_0 = 300 \, K$, $K = 7.5 \times 10^{-9} \, V \cdot K^{-4}$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$, $\varepsilon = 0.8$.

**To find:** True temperature $T_t$.

**Note:** The instrument constant $K$ already incorporates the emissivity factor (since the pyrometer is calibrated for a specific emissivity). If the problem states $K$ is for an arbitrary body, we use the equation:

$$
e = K \cdot \sigma (T_t^4 - T_0^4)
$$

**Step 1:** Compute $T_0^4 = 300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9} \, K^4$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the EMF equation:

$$
T_t^4 - T_0^4 = \dfrac{e}{K \sigma}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $K \sigma = (7.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) = 4.2525 \times 10^{-16} \, V \cdot W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$

Wait — note that $e$ is in volts, $K$ in V·K⁻⁴, $\sigma$ in W/(m²·K⁴). The product $K\sigma$ has dimensions of (V·K⁻⁴)·(W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴). For the equation to be dimensionally consistent, $K$ here is interpreted as a *normalized* instrument constant combining all geometric and material factors into a single V·K⁴/W·m⁻² type ratio. For the purpose of this numerical problem, we treat $K\sigma$ as a single product and verify via magnitudes.

Let $K' = K \sigma = 4.2525 \times 10^{-16} \, V \cdot K^{-4} \cdot W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$

**Step 4:** $T_t^4 - T_0^4 = \dfrac{4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4.2525 \times 10^{-16}} = 9.876 \times 10^{12} \, K^4$ (treating the combined units)

**Step 5:** $T_t^4 = 9.876 \times 10^{12} + 8.1 \times 10^{9} = 9.876 \times 10^{12} + 0.0081 \times 10^{12} = 9.884 \times 10^{12} \, K^4$

**Step 6:** $T_t = (9.884 \times 10^{12})^{1/4}$

Compute the fourth root: $(9.884 \times 10^{12})^{1/2} = \sqrt{9.884} \times 10^6 = 3.144 \times 10^6$

Then take square root again: $\sqrt{3.144 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{3.144} \times 10^3 = 1.773 \times 10^3 = 1773 \, K$

**Step 7:** Convert to °C: $T_t = 1773 - 273.15 \approx 1500 \, °C$

**Final Answer:** The true temperature of the furnace is **T_t ≈ 1773 K (≈ 1500 °C)**.

> *(Note: $T_0^4$ is negligible compared to $T_t^4$ in this case — only 0.08 % — confirming the engineering approximation often used.)*

> *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark formula, 1 mark rearrangement, 1 mark Kσ calculation, 2 marks for $T_t^4$ computation, 1 mark fourth root, 1 mark final answer with units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert λ to meters.** The most common error in optical pyrometer problems: leaving λ in μm. **Always convert to meters** before plugging into the formula (since $C_2$ is in m·K).
> 2. **Mixing up brightness temperature and true temperature.** The pyrometer *reads* $T_b$, not $T$. Always apply the emissivity correction equation to obtain true $T$.
> 3. **Forgetting the cold-junction term in radiation pyrometer.** $T_0^4$ is often small but cannot be omitted in formal solutions — show it explicitly.
> 4. **Using °C in Stefan-Boltzmann law.** This is a *fatal* error. **Always use Kelvin (K)** for all temperatures in $T^4$ equations.
> 5. **Skipping the $\varepsilon$ factor for real bodies.** Blackbody equations assume $\varepsilon = 1$. For real bodies, $\varepsilon$ must appear — losing 2 marks otherwise.
> 6. **Not stating units in the final answer.** KTU 2024 scheme strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- 🔹 **Pyrometer = non-contact high-T measurement** (typically > 600 °C).
- 🔹 **Optical Pyrometer** uses **monochromatic brightness comparison** at **λ ≈ 0.65 μm** (red filter), with a **tungsten filament** as reference. **Eye is the detector.**
- 🔹 **Disappearing filament** = brightness match between filament and target image.
- 🔹 **Radiation Pyrometer** uses **total thermal radiation** (all wavelengths) collected and focused on a **thermopile**.
- 🔹 **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $E_b = \sigma T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).
- 🔹 **Wien's Displacement Law**: $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2898 \, \mu m \cdot K$.
- 🔹 **Wien's Approximation** (used in optical pyrometer): $E_{b\lambda} \approx C_1 \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$, valid for $\lambda T < 3000 \, \mu m \cdot K$.
- 🔹 **Brightness Temperature Correction**: $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln(1/\varepsilon_\lambda)$.
- 🔹 **Radiation Pyrometer EMF**: $e = K \sigma (T_t^4 - T_0^4)$ — fourth-power dependence on target temperature.
- 🔹 **Always use Kelvin** in $T^4$ equations; convert to °C only for final display.
- 🔹 **Optical Pyrometer = subjective** (operator eye); **Radiation Pyrometer = objective** (electrical output).
- 🔹 **Optical range: 600–3000 °C; Radiation range: 600–4000 °C.**
- 🔹 **Calibration source** for both: **blackbody furnace**.
- 🔹 **Emissivity correction** is mandatory for real (non-blackbody) targets in both pyrometer types.
- 🔹 **C₂ constant**: $1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K — memorize this value.
- 🔹 **Radiation pyrometer has a fourth-power sensitivity** — extremely responsive to small T changes at high T.
- 🔹 **Application areas:** Steel plants, foundries, glass furnaces, cement kilns, ceramic firing, turbine blade monitoring, induction heating, semiconductor processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working and Torque equations of PMMC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEIT304 - SENSORS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working and Torque equations of PMMC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Instrument** is a *deflection-type* analog measuring instrument in which a rectangular coil carrying the current to be measured is suspended (or pivoted) in the uniform radial magnetic field of a permanent magnet. The interaction between the coil current and the permanent magnetic field produces a deflecting torque proportional to the current, and this deflection is opposed by a control torque supplied by spiral springs. The pointer deflection is therefore a direct measure of the current through the coil.

Formally, it is also called a **D'Arsonval movement** or **Galvanometer movement** and is the most accurate DC measuring instrument used as the *movement* inside analog ammeters, voltmeters, ohmmeters, and DC signal-conditioning circuits.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Quick Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PMMC** | Permanent Magnet Moving Coil |
| **D'Arsonval Movement** | Historical name of the PMMC mechanism |
| **Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)** | Torque produced by the current–magnetic-field interaction |
| **Control Torque ($T_c$)** | Restoring torque produced by the spiral spring |
| **Damping Torque ($T_D$)** | Torque that suppresses oscillations (eddy-current damping here) |
| **Radial Magnetic Field** | Field always tangential to the coil sides, keeping force constant over the deflection arc |
| **Aluminium Former** | Non-magnetic former on which the coil is wound; provides eddy-current damping |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnetic See-Saw"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a tiny rectangular loop of wire sitting between the poles of a strong permanent horseshoe magnet. The moment you push DC current through the loop, the two *long sides* of the loop experience equal and opposite forces (one pushed up, one pushed down) because of **F = BIL**. Since these forces are separated by a small distance, they create a twisting motion — exactly like two children sitting on opposite ends of a see-saw, pushing the beam to rotate.

The pointer attached to the coil rotates. But as it rotates, a tiny **spiral hair-spring** is wound tighter, pulling the pointer back. The pointer comes to rest exactly when the magnetic push (deflecting torque) equals the spring pull (control torque). The final angular position is therefore *linearly proportional* to the current — this is why PMMC has a **uniform scale**.

## 1.4 Why PMMC? — Salient Features

> [!TIP]
> - Measures **only DC** (average value of current/voltage).
> - Very **high accuracy** (typically ±2% to ±5% of full-scale).
> - **Linear (uniform) scale** because $\theta \propto I$.
> - **High sensitivity** because of strong Alnico / rare-earth permanent magnets.
> - **High torque-to-weight ratio** → suitable for portable instruments.
> - **Low power consumption** (typically 25 µW to 200 µW).
> - Constructionally robust; not affected by stray magnetic fields due to the closed iron core.

## 1.5 Limitations to Remember

> [!WARNING]
> - **Cannot measure AC** directly — the average torque over a full AC cycle is zero, so the pointer rests at zero.
> - More **expensive** than MI (moving iron) instruments.
> - The coil current must be limited to a safe value (otherwise springs burn out).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of PMMC — The Six Building Blocks

1. **Permanent Magnet System**
   - High-coercivity magnets (historically Alnico, now Alnico-V or rare-earth).
   - Pole pieces are shaped to produce a **cylindrical (radial) air-gap** so that the magnetic field is always perpendicular to the coil sides along the arc of motion.
2. **Moving Coil**
   - Rectangular coil of insulated copper wire wound on an aluminium former.
   - Typically 10–20 turns for voltmeters, more turns for ammeters/galvanometers.
3. **Aluminium Former (Damping Frame)**
   - The rectangular former is a closed aluminium loop. As the coil rotates, eddy currents induced in this frame provide **eddy-current damping** — no separate damping mechanism is required.
4. **Control System — Spiral Springs**
   - Two phosphor-bronze spiral hairsprings, wound in opposite directions, provide both the control torque *and* act as current lead-in to the coil.
5. **Pointer and Scale**
   - Lightweight aluminium pointer attached to the spindle. Scale is uniformly graduated.
6. **Damping, Balance & Shielding**
   - Eddy-current damping (above). Counter-weights balance the moving system. Magnetic shielding (mu-metal) avoids stray field pickup.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The operating principle relies on the **Lorentz force law**: a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force $F = BIL$ where the force direction is given by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule.

**Step 1 — Current Entry.** The current to be measured enters the moving coil through one of the control springs.

**Step 2 — Force Generation.** Each vertical side of the rectangular coil lies in the **radial magnetic field** of the permanent magnet. Because the field is radial, it is always tangential to the coil sides, so the force $F = BIL$ is **always perpendicular to the active side** for the entire deflection arc.

**Step 3 — Torque Production.** The two vertical sides carry current in opposite directions, so forces act in **opposite directions** on the two sides. The total deflecting torque is the sum of the two forces times the moment arm (the horizontal side length, i.e., the coil width).

**Step 4 — Spring Opposition.** As the coil rotates, the spiral spring is wound tighter, producing a restoring torque proportional to the angle of deflection: $T_c \propto \theta$.

**Step 5 — Equilibrium.** The coil comes to rest when $T_d = T_c$. Because the radial field keeps $F$ constant over the deflection range, the relationship $\theta \propto I$ is **linear**, giving a uniform scale.

**Step 6 — Damping.** The aluminium former rotates in the magnetic field; eddy currents induced in it produce a torque opposing the motion (Lenz's law), rapidly bringing the pointer to steady deflection without oscillation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> PMMC movements are the *backbone* of every analog panel meter and DC measurement transducer. They appear inside:
> - DC Ammeters and Voltmeters (with multiplier/shunt resistors).
> - Analog multimeters (VOM).
> - Ohm-meters, galvanometers.
> - **Transducer-based readouts** — e.g., the D'Arsonval movement driven by a Wheatstone-bridge output from a strain gauge, RTD, or thermocouple amplifier.
> - Process-industry panel indicators fed by 4–20 mA loops (after I-to-V conversion).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorise formula set** for any PMMC question in the KTU 2024 scheme examination. Units are indicated in square brackets.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Force on a coil side | $F$ | $F = B \, I \, L$ | $\text{[N]}$ |
| 2 | Total Deflecting Torque | $T_d$ | $T_d = N \, B \, I \, A$ | $\text{[N·m]}$ |
| 3 | Spring Control Torque | $T_c$ | $T_c = K \, \theta$ | $\text{[N·m]}$ |
| 4 | Eddy-current Damping Torque | $T_D$ | $T_D = D \, \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | $\text{[N·m]}$ |
| 5 | Steady-state Deflection | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{N \, B \, A}{K} \, I$ | $\text{[rad]}$ |
| 6 | Current Sensitivity | $S_I$ | $S_I = \dfrac{\theta}{I} = \dfrac{N B A}{K}$ | $\text{[rad/A]}$ |
| 7 | Power Consumed at FSD | $P$ | $P = I_{fs}^2 \, R_m$ | $\text{[W]}$ |
| 8 | Voltmeter Multiplier | $R_s$ | $R_s = (m - 1) \, R_m,\; m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ | $\text{[Ω]}$ |
| 9 | Ammeter Shunt | $R_{sh}$ | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1},\; m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$ | $\text{[Ω]}$ |

> Where:
> - $N$ = number of turns of the moving coil,
> - $B$ = flux density in the air gap $\text{[Wb/m}^2\text{ or T]}$,
> - $I$ = current through the coil $\text{[A]}$,
> - $A$ = effective coil face area $= l \times d$ $\text{[m}^2\text{]}$,
> - $K$ = spring constant $\text{[N·m/rad]}$,
> - $\theta$ = angular deflection $\text{[rad]}$,
> - $D$ = damping constant $\text{[N·m·s/rad]}$,
> - $m$ = multiplying factor (ratio of full-scale to meter full-scale current/voltage).

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **absolute-value rule** in tables: any reference to magnitude is written as $\vert x \vert$, never as $\vert x \vert$ directly inside a markdown table cell (to avoid breaking column formatting).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)

Consider a rectangular coil of $N$ turns with horizontal length $l$ (vertical sides) and vertical width $d$ (horizontal sides), suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field of flux density $B$ established between the pole pieces and a soft-iron core.

**Step 1 — Force on a single vertical side of the coil.** Each vertical side of length $l$ carries current $I$ and lies fully inside the magnetic field. The force experienced by *one* vertical side is:

$$
F_{side} = B \, I \, l
$$

The factor is the Lorentz force on a straight conductor of length $l$ in field $B$.

**Step 2 — Force on both vertical sides.** Because the two vertical sides carry current in *opposite directions* (current goes up on one side and down on the other), the forces on the two sides are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. Hence they form a **couple**.

**Step 3 — Moment arm.** The two equal and opposite forces are separated by a perpendicular distance equal to the coil width $d$. The torque produced by this couple is force $\times$ perpendicular distance:

$$
T_{one\ turn} = F_{side} \times d = B \, I \, l \times d
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the area.** Since the coil is rectangular with dimensions $l \times d$, the enclosed area is $A = l \times d$. So:

$$
T_{one\ turn} = B \, I \, A
$$

**Step 5 — Account for all turns.** With $N$ identical turns wound on the same former, each turn contributes equally to the torque, and the turns are effectively in parallel magnetically (same flux links each turn). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T_d = N \, B \, I \, A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because the field is **radial**, the angle between $B$ and the coil-side current direction is always $90°$ for the entire deflection range — that is why $T_d$ is *independent of $\theta$*. This is the geometric reason for the linear scale.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Control Torque ($T_c$)

The spiral control spring obeys Hooke's law in torsion. When twisted through an angle $\theta$, it produces a restoring torque proportional to $\theta$:

$$
\boxed{T_c = K \, \theta}
$$

where $K$ is the **spring constant** in N·m/rad. The spring is designed to be linear over the deflection range.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Damping Torque ($T_D$)

In PMMC, damping is provided by the closed aluminium former rotating in the radial magnetic field.

**Step 1 — EMF induced in the former.** As the coil (and hence the former) rotates at angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$, an EMF is induced in the former loop:

$$
e_{former} = B \, A_f \, \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

where $A_f$ is the effective area of the former.

**Step 2 — Eddy current.** Because the former is a closed conducting loop, this EMF drives an eddy current $i_e = e_{former} / R_f$, where $R_f$ is the resistance of the former loop.

**Step 3 — Torque produced by the eddy current.** The eddy current flowing through the former in the magnetic field experiences the same Lorentz force as the main coil. The torque produced by the eddy current is:

$$
T_D = \frac{B \, A_f}{R_f} \cdot B \, A_f \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine constants.** Grouping $B^2 A_f^2 / R_f$ as a single constant $D$:

$$
\boxed{T_D = D \, \frac{d\theta}{dt}}
$$

This torque always opposes the motion (Lenz's law) and brings the moving system to rest without oscillation. $D$ is called the **damping constant**.

---

## 3.4 The Complete Dynamic Equation of Motion

Newton's second law for rotation of the moving system is:

$$
J \, \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = \sum \text{(torques)}
$$

where $J$ is the moment of inertia of the coil assembly. The driving torque is $T_d$, the spring provides $-T_c$, and the eddy former provides $-T_D$:

$$
J \, \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \, \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K \, \theta = N \, B \, I \, A
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **second-order linear differential equation with constant coefficients**. Its behaviour depends on the damping ratio:
> - $\zeta < 1$ — underdamped (oscillatory approach to steady state).
> - $\zeta = 1$ — critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory response; ideal design target).
> - $\zeta > 1$ — overdamped (sluggish).

---

## 3.5 Steady-State Deflection (DC Measurement)

Under **DC conditions** ($\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = 0$ after transients die out), the dynamic equation reduces to:

$$
K \, \theta_{ss} = N \, B \, I \, A
$$

Solving for the steady-state angular deflection:

$$
\boxed{\theta_{ss} = \frac{N \, B \, A}{K} \, I}
$$

Since $N$, $B$, $A$, and $K$ are constants for a given instrument, $\theta_{ss} \propto I$. The proportionality constant $\dfrac{NBA}{K}$ is the **current sensitivity** $S_I$.

$$
\boxed{S_I = \frac{\theta_{ss}}{I} = \frac{N \, B \, A}{K}}
$$

The **current** required to produce a full-scale deflection $\theta_{fs}$ is:

$$
\boxed{I_{fs} = \frac{K \, \theta_{fs}}{N \, B \, A}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Why PMMC Cannot Measure AC

For an AC current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, the instantaneous torque is $T_d(t) = N B A \, I_m \sin(\omega t)$. The moving system has a large mechanical time constant — it cannot follow the high-frequency oscillation. The pointer responds to the **average torque over a cycle**:

$$
\langle T_d \rangle = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T N B A \, I_m \sin(\omega t) \, dt = 0
$$

So the average torque is zero, and the pointer returns to its mechanical zero — the meter reads **zero** for pure AC. (With AC + DC offset, it reads the DC component, which is why a PMMC with a rectifier is used as an AC voltmeter — but that belongs to a later module.)

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick Numerical Reasoning

> [!TIP]
> To intuitively *size* the variables in a PMMC problem, remember the trade-offs:

| Want higher sensitivity $S_I = NBA/K$ | Consequence |
|---|---|
| Increase $N$ | More turns → larger coil mass, more inertia, slower response |
| Increase $B$ | Stronger magnet needed; bigger, heavier, costlier |
| Increase $A$ | Larger coil; again more inertia |
| Decrease $K$ | Softer spring → weaker control → more prone to vibration/shock error |

This is why designing a PMMC is a **multi-objective optimisation problem**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Construction Layout — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **physical components** of a PMMC and shows how the **signal path** (current) and **torque path** (mechanical) interact to produce a pointer deflection.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[DC Current Source I] --> sprA[Upper Hair Spring]
    sprA --> coilA[Moving Coil N turns wound on Aluminium Former]
    coilA -. Force F equals B I L .-> fldA[Radial Magnetic Field B from Permanent Magnet]
    fldA --> tqA[Deflecting Torque Td equals N B I A]
    tqA --> spdA[Spindle rotates by angle theta]
    spdA --> ptA[Aluminium Pointer]
    ptA --> sclA[Uniform Scale deflection theta proportional to I]
    spdA --> edA[Eddy currents induced in Aluminium Former]
    edA --> tqD[Damping Torque TD equals D dtheta by dt opposes motion]
    spdA --> sprB[Lower Hair Spring twists and stores energy]
    sprB --> tqC[Control Torque Tc equals K theta restores pointer]
    tqC --> spdA
    ptA -. Equilibrium reached .-> eqA[When Td equals Tc plus TD steady state]
```

## 4.2 Functional Torque Flow Diagram

The figure below shows the **dynamic balance** of torques acting on the moving system, as derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Current I] --> defA[Deflecting Torque Td equals N B I A]
    springA[Spring action] --> ctrlA[Control Torque Tc equals K theta]
    formerA[Aluminium former eddy currents] --> dampA[Damping Torque TD equals D dtheta by dt]
    defA --> sumA[Net Torque drives spindle]
    ctrlA --> sumA
    dampA --> sumA
    sumA --> outA[Pointer deflection theta equals NBA by K times I]
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Electrical Circuit of the Moving Coil

The moving coil has a small resistance $R_m$ and a small inductance $L_m$. The terminal voltage equation is:

$$
v_m(t) = R_m \, i(t) + L_m \, \frac{di(t)}{dt} + e_{back}
$$

where $e_{back} = N B A \, \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ is the **back-EMF** induced by the coil rotating in the magnetic field (the *electromechanical* counterpart of the eddy-current damping equation).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    termA[Terminals A B] --> rm[Coil Resistance Rm]
    rm --> lm[Coil Inductance Lm]
    lm --> bemf[Back EMF equals N B A dtheta by dt]
    bemf --> termB[Terminals A B closed loop]
```

## 4.4 Critical Damping Design Target

Designers tune $D$ (via the former thickness) so that the damping ratio $\zeta$ lies between 0.7 and 1.0 for a fast, oscillation-free pointer response.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    desA[Damping Ratio zeta] --> |zeta less than 1| und[Underdamped pointer oscillates]
    desA --> |zeta equals 1| crit[Critically damped fast no oscillation ideal]
    desA --> |zeta greater than 1| ovr[Overdamped pointer sluggish]
    crit --> desB[Design Target for PMMC]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define deflecting torque, control torque and damping torque as applied to PMMC instruments. State the physical mechanism that produces each torque in a D'Arsonval movement.**

*Model Answer (expected length ≈ 6–8 lines):*

- **Deflecting Torque ($T_d$):** The torque produced by the interaction of the current $I$ flowing through the moving coil and the radial magnetic field $B$ of the permanent magnet. Its magnitude is $T_d = N B I A$. It is the *driving* torque that tends to rotate the coil.
- **Control Torque ($T_c$):** The restoring torque produced by the spiral control springs. It is proportional to the angular deflection: $T_c = K \theta$. It opposes the deflecting torque and brings the coil to a definite rest position.
- **Damping Torque ($T_D$):** The torque that opposes the *motion* of the coil, produced by **eddy currents induced in the closed aluminium former** as it rotates in the magnetic field. Its magnitude is $T_D = D (d\theta/dt)$. It is responsible for quickly bringing the pointer to steady-state without oscillation.

> **[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks]**

---

**Q2. Why does a PMMC instrument have a uniform (linear) scale, and why is it not suitable for measuring pure AC quantities?**

*Model Answer:*

The radial (cylindrical) air-gap ensures that the magnetic field $B$ is always perpendicular to the active coil sides over the entire range of deflection. Consequently, the deflecting torque $T_d = N B I A$ is *independent of $\theta$*, and the steady-state deflection $\theta = (NBA/K) I$ is directly proportional to $I$. This yields a **uniform scale**.

For AC, $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, the instantaneous torque oscillates symmetrically about zero. The high-inertia moving system responds only to the **average torque**, which is zero over a complete cycle. Hence the pointer reads **zero** for AC — PMMC is therefore restricted to DC measurement (or rectified AC with an external rectifier).

> **[Marks Distribution: 1.5 for uniform scale reason + 1.5 for AC limitation = 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ➤ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument. Derive the expression for the deflecting torque and show that the steady-state deflection is proportional to the current.
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A PMMC voltmeter has a coil resistance of $100\ \Omega$ and a full-scale deflection current of $50\ \mu A$. It is to be converted to read **(i)** $0$–$10\ \text{V}$ and **(ii)** $0$–$250\ \text{V}$. Calculate the required multiplier resistance in each case and the multiplying factor. Comment on the power consumption in each range.
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

1. **Construction (≈ 3 marks).** List the six construction blocks from Section 2.1 (magnet system, moving coil, aluminium former, springs, pointer/scale, damping/balance/shielding). A labelled sketch should show: permanent magnet (with curved pole pieces), soft-iron cylindrical core, rectangular coil wound on former, two hairsprings, spindle with pointer.

2. **Working (≈ 1.5 marks).** When the current to be measured flows through the coil, each vertical side of length $l$ in the radial field $B$ experiences force $F = B I l$. The two sides form a couple producing deflection. The pointer rotates against the spring until equilibrium.

3. **Derivation of $T_d$ (≈ 2 marks).** Reproduce the derivation from Section 3.1:
   - $F = B I l$ on each vertical side.
   - Couple = $F \times d = B I l d = B I A$ (since $A = l d$).
   - With $N$ turns: $T_d = N B I A$.

4. **Steady-state proportionality (≈ 0.5 mark).** Show $\theta = (NBA/K) I \Rightarrow \theta \propto I$.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Given:** $R_m = 100\ \Omega$, $I_{fs} = 50\ \mu\text{A} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$.

> The full-scale voltage of the basic meter movement itself is:
> $$V_m = I_{fs} \times R_m = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100 = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 5\ \text{mV}$$

**Case (i) — Range $V = 10\ \text{V}$.**

The multiplying factor is:

$$
m_1 = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{10}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2000
$$

The series multiplier resistance:

$$
R_{s1} = (m_1 - 1) \, R_m = (2000 - 1) \times 100 = 1999 \times 100 = 1.999 \times 10^{5}\ \Omega \approx 199.9\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Case (ii) — Range $V = 250\ \text{V}$.**

$$
m_2 = \frac{250}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 50{,}000
$$

$$
R_{s2} = (m_2 - 1) \times 100 = 49{,}999 \times 100 = 4.9999 \times 10^{6}\ \Omega \approx 5\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

**Power Consumption at FSD:**

For Case (i): $P_1 = V \times I_{fs} = 10 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W} = 0.5\ \text{mW}$.

For Case (ii): $P_2 = 250 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 1.25 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{W} = 12.5\ \text{mW}$.

**Comment:** Although PMMC movements consume *very little power* at the basic $5\ \text{mV}$ level ($2.5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{W}$), the *external circuit* must dissipate additional power in the multiplier. The higher the voltage range, the larger the multiplier and the higher the total power consumption — yet the *measurement* power drawn by the meter movement remains $0.25\ \mu\text{W}$.

> **[Marks Distribution: 1.5 for $V_m$ + 2 for case (i) + 2 for case (ii) + 1.5 for power/comment = 7 marks]**

---

### ➤ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Derive the dynamic equation of motion of a PMMC instrument and identify each term. Explain the roles of damping torque and control torque in determining the steady-state and transient behaviour.
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A PMMC ammeter has a coil resistance of $50\ \Omega$ and full-scale deflection current of $15\ \text{mA}$. It is to be used to measure currents up to **(i)** $5\ \text{A}$ and **(ii)** $50\ \text{A}$. Calculate the required shunt resistance in each case. What is the effect on accuracy if the temperature changes and how is it mitigated?
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Dynamic equation of motion (≈ 3 marks derivation):** Starting from Newton's second law for rotation, with the three torques acting on the moving system:

$$
J \, \frac{d^{2}\theta}{d t^{2}} = T_d - T_c - T_D
$$

Substituting the three torque expressions derived earlier:

$$
J \, \frac{d^{2}\theta}{d t^{2}} + D \, \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K \, \theta = N \, B \, I \, A
$$

Identify each term:

- $J \, \dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2}$ — **inertia term** (Newton's second law).
- $D \, \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ — **damping term** (eddy-current damping).
- $K \, \theta$ — **spring restoring term** (control torque).
- $N B I A$ — **driving/deflecting term** (input forcing).

**Roles of each torque (≈ 4 marks):**

- **Deflecting torque** $T_d = N B I A$: *drives* the coil from rest, proportional to the input current.
- **Control torque** $T_c = K \theta$: *limits* the deflection to a fixed value proportional to $I$; without it the coil would swing to the mechanical stop. Determines the **steady-state** value: $\theta_{ss} = (NBA/K) I$.
- **Damping torque** $T_D = D (d\theta/dt)$: controls the **transient** response (how quickly and how smoothly the pointer reaches steady state). Critical damping ($\zeta = 1$) is the design target.

**Transient analysis (≈ 1 mark mention):** Damping ratio:

$$
\zeta = \frac{D}{2 \sqrt{J K}}
$$

For $\zeta < 1$ the response oscillates; for $\zeta > 1$ it is sluggish; $\zeta = 1$ is optimal.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Given:** $R_m = 50\ \Omega$, $I_m = I_{fs} = 15\ \text{mA} = 0.015\ \text{A}$.

The shunt resistor $R_{sh}$ is connected in **parallel** with the meter movement to divert the excess current $I - I_m$.

For a multiplying factor $m = I / I_m$, the shunt is:

$$
R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}
$$

**Case (i) — Range $I = 5\ \text{A}$.**

$$
m_1 = \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{5}{0.015} = \frac{5000}{15} = 333.\overline{3}
$$

$$
R_{sh1} = \frac{50}{333.33 - 1} = \frac{50}{332.33} \approx 0.1504\ \Omega
$$

**Case (ii) — Range $I = 50\ \text{A}$.**

$$
m_2 = \frac{50}{0.015} = \frac{50{,}000}{15} \approx 3333.\overline{3}
$$

$$
R_{sh2} = \frac{50}{3332.33} \approx 0.0150\ \Omega
$$

**Temperature effects and mitigation (≈ 2 marks):**

The coil is made of copper, whose resistance rises with temperature (≈ $+0.4\%/^{\circ}\text{C}$). When temperature rises:

- The share of current flowing through the meter coil decreases (because $R_m$ rises relative to $R_{sh}$), so the pointer reads **low** (error is negative).
- The spring constant $K$ also drifts slightly with temperature.

**Mitigation (Swamp Resistor / Swamping):** A **manganin** shunt (temperature coefficient ≈ $\pm 0.002\%/^{\circ}\text{C}$, very low) is used in series with the copper coil such that the total meter-branch resistance is dominated by the temperature-stable series resistor. The combined temperature coefficient of the meter branch approaches that of manganin, drastically reducing temperature error. Additionally, a **Swamp resistor** $R_{sw}$ in series with the coil of $R_{sw} \gg R_m$ ensures most of the meter-branch resistance is temperature-independent.

> **[Marks Distribution: 1.5 for formula + 1.5 for case (i) + 1.5 for case (ii) + 2.5 for temperature effects/mitigation = 7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
