| Analogy Element | Real World | Spectrum Analyzer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| White light | Mixed complex signal | Input signal |
| Prism | Splits light by wavelength | Internal heterodyne mixing / FFT |
| Rainbow (colors) | Individual frequency components | Displayed spectrum |
| Brightness of each color | Loudness / intensity | Amplitude (Y-axis) |
| Position of each color | Pitch / frequency | Frequency (X-axis) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The spectrum analyzer is the *time-domain counterpart* of an oscilloscope. An oscilloscope shows **amplitude vs. time**, whereas a spectrum analyzer shows **amplitude vs. frequency**. This distinction is a favorite KTU question!

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- The **dBm** unit: $0\,\text{dBm} = 1\,\text{mW}$ dissipated in a **$50\,\Omega$** standard reference load.
- The **dBV** unit: $0\,\text{dBV} = 1\,\text{V}_{\text{RMS}}$ reference.
- Standard input impedance: **$50\,\Omega$** (most RF analyzers) or **$75\,\Omega$** (video/cable TV systems).
- The instrument operates in the **frequency range** from as low as **$9\,\text{kHz}$** to as high as **$50\,\text{GHz}$** (and beyond in millimeter-wave versions).
- **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** typically ranges from **$1\,\text{Hz}$** to **$3\,\text{MHz}$** in stepped increments (1, 3, 10 sequence).

> [!TIP]
> **Why $50\,\Omega$?** It is the geometric mean of the characteristic impedances of free space ($\approx 377\,\Omega$) and a copper waveguide ($\approx 22\,\Omega$) in air — a practical compromise that minimizes both signal loss and power-handling limitations in RF systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Spectrum Analyzers (KTU-Relevant Taxonomy)

Modern spectrum analyzers fall into three major architectural families:

### 2.1.1 Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne) Spectrum Analyzer
This is the **classic, most commonly examined architecture** in KTU papers. It works on the principle of mixing the input signal with a locally generated sweeping oscillator (Local Oscillator, LO) to produce a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF), which is then filtered, detected, and displayed.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Input RF signal is attenuated by a **variable input attenuator** to prevent overloading.
2. The signal passes through a **low-pass pre-filter** to reject out-of-band images.
3. A **mixer** multiplies the input with a sweeping LO output: $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{signal} \vert$.
4. The resulting IF signal is filtered by a **Resolution Bandwidth Filter (RBW filter)**.
5. The filtered IF is **envelope-detected** (log or linear).
6. A **video filter** smooths the detected envelope.
7. The amplitude is plotted on the Y-axis; the LO sweep voltage drives the X-axis, creating the frequency-domain display.

### 2.1.2 FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) Spectrum Analyzer
- Captures a finite time-domain block of the input signal using a fast **A/D converter**.
- Computes the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** — practically implemented via the Cooley-Tukey FFT algorithm.
- The result is converted to magnitude and displayed on a logarithmic (or linear) amplitude scale.
- **Best suited for:** baseband signals, audio, vibration analysis, low-frequency measurements.

### 2.1.3 Real-Time Spectrum Analyzer (RTSA)
- Uses parallel digital processing to capture and display *all* frequency components simultaneously with no sweep-time blind spot.
- **Best suited for:** transient signals (e.g., radar pulses, EMI debugging, frequency-hopping radios).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (No Vertical Pipe Characters)

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Heterodyne (Mixing) Law | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{signal} \vert$ | Hz | Defines the down-conversion |
| 2 | Frequency Span | $Span = f_{stop} - f_{start}$ | Hz | Total displayed frequency range |
| 3 | Sweep Time (approximate) | $T_{sw} \approx \dfrac{Span}{RBW \cdot k}$ | seconds | $k$ depends on filter shape factor |
| 4 | Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) | $RBW = 3\,\text{dB\,bandwidth\,of\,IF\,filter}$ | Hz | Selectivity of the analyzer |
| 5 | Video Bandwidth (VBW) | $VBW \le RBW$ | Hz | Smoothing post-detection filter |
| 6 | dBm Conversion | $P(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\,\text{mW}}\right)$ | dBm | Standard amplitude unit |
| 7 | dBV Conversion | $V(\text{dBV}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{RMS}}{1\,\text{V}}\right)$ | dBV | For voltage-displayed analyzers |
| 8 | Voltage from dBm (50 Ω) | $V = \sqrt{P \cdot 50\,\Omega}$ | Volts | Used in sensitivity calculations |
| 9 | Sensitivity (noise floor) | $DANL = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$ | dBm/Hz | Thermal noise floor + noise figure |
| 10 | Shape Factor of IF filter | $SF = \dfrac{BW_{60dB}}{BW_{3dB}}$ | unitless | Ideal $\approx 4:1$; Gaussian $\approx 4.6:1$ |
| 11 | Image Frequency Rejection | $f_{image} = f_{signal} \pm 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz | Pre-filter must reject this |
| 12 | Phase Noise density | $\mathcal{L}(f) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{noise\,per\,Hz}}{P_{carrier}}\right)$ | dBc/Hz | Measured at offset $f$ from carrier |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**Three Pairs that must be coupled**": (1) Span $\leftrightarrow$ RBW, (2) RBW $\leftrightarrow$ Sweep Time, (3) RBW $\leftrightarrow$ VBW. Coupling these correctly is what separates a good display from a meaningless one.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Verifying carrier frequencies, sideband symmetry, and modulation depth in transmitters.
- **EMI/EMC Compliance Testing:** Detecting spurious emissions from digital circuits and certifying products against CISPR/FCC limits.
- **Radar & Defense:** Analyzing pulse repetition frequencies, chirp linearity, and Doppler shifts.
- **Audio & Acoustics:** Measuring harmonic distortion in amplifiers, speaker resonance peaks.
- **Medical Electronics:** Analyzing bio-electric signals (ECG, EMG) in the frequency domain.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Detecting bearing faults, shaft misalignment in rotating machinery via vibration frequency spectra.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice between **swept-tuned** and **FFT** analyzer is governed by the input frequency. Swept-tuned analyzers can handle GHz signals because the A/D converter is never asked to digitize RF directly — the heterodyne mixer does the frequency shift. FFT analyzers are limited by A/D sampling rate (Nyquist) to lower frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heterodyne Mixing Law and IF Generation

**Starting Point:** The mixer is a non-linear device whose output contains the sum and difference frequencies of the two inputs.

**Step 1 — Define the input signal and the local oscillator output.**

$$v_s(t) = A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t)$$

$$v_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 2 — The mixer multiplies the two signals (ideal multiplier).**

$$v_{mixer}(t) = v_s(t) \cdot v_{LO}(t) = A_s A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_s t) \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**

$$\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(\alpha-\beta) + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\bigr]$$

$$v_{mixer}(t) = \tfrac{A_s A_{LO}}{2}\Bigl[\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{LO}-f_s)t\bigr) + \cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{LO}+f_s)t\bigr)\Bigr]$$

**Step 4 — The IF bandpass filter passes only the difference frequency (lower sideband).**

$$v_{IF}(t) = \tfrac{A_s A_{LO}}{2}\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{LO}-f_s)t\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — The IF frequency is therefore the absolute difference of the two inputs.**

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{s} \vert$$

This is the **fundamental heterodyne equation** that underpins the entire swept-tuned spectrum analyzer architecture.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sweep Time Approximation

**Step 1 — The IF filter is swept through the band of interest.** Each spectral component spends a time $\tau$ inside the passband, where $\tau$ is approximately the filter's impulse response time.

**Step 2 — Filter impulse response time is inversely proportional to its 3-dB bandwidth.**

$$\tau \approx \frac{1}{RBW}$$

**Step 3 — Total sweep time is the number of filter traversals (Span / RBW) times the dwell time.**

$$T_{sw} = \left(\frac{Span}{RBW}\right) \cdot \tau$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $\tau$ to obtain the KTU formula.**

$$T_{sw} = \frac{Span}{RBW} \cdot \frac{1}{RBW} \cdot k = \frac{k \cdot Span}{RBW^2}$$

For a Gaussian filter, the constant $k \approx 2.5$, so the practical KTU-ready expression is:

$$T_{sw} \approx 2.5 \cdot \frac{Span}{RBW^2}$$

This is why **reducing the RBW by 10× increases the sweep time by 100×** — a critical trade-off in measurement setup.

## 3.3 Derivation: Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL / Sensitivity)

**Step 1 — Thermal noise power available from any source at temperature $T$.**

$$P_N = k_B T B$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant) and $B$ is the noise-equivalent bandwidth.

**Step 2 — Reference at standard temperature $T_0 = 290\,\text{K}$.** The product $k_B T_0 = 4 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{W/Hz}$, which gives the well-known noise floor:

$$P_N \text{ in dBm} = -174\,\text{dBm/Hz} + 10 \log_{10}(B)$$

**Step 3 — Add the noise figure (NF) of the analyzer's front-end.**

$$DANL = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For a spectrum analyzer with $NF = 20\,\text{dB}$ and $RBW = 10\,\text{kHz}$:
> 
> $$DANL = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(10\,000) + 20 = -174 + 40 + 20 = -114\,\text{dBm}$$
> 
> This means the analyzer cannot reliably detect signals weaker than $-114\,\text{dBm}$ with a $10\,\text{kHz}$ resolution filter.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favorite: Spectrum Analyzer vs. Oscilloscope)

| Feature | Oscilloscope | Spectrum Analyzer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary Domain | Time | Frequency |
| X-axis quantity | Time ($s$) | Frequency (Hz) |
| Y-axis quantity | Voltage (V) | Power (dBm) or Voltage (dBV) |
| Phase information | Yes (via triggering) | No (phase lost in envelope detection) |
| Frequency resolution | Poor for high-freq signals | Excellent (down to sub-Hz RBW) |
| Sensitivity to small signals | Limited by ADC noise | Excellent (down to $-170\,\text{dBm}$) |
| Best for | Time-domain transients | Harmonic, noise, distortion analysis |
| Typical use case | Digital logic, switching waveforms | RF, microwave, communications |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyzer

The figure below is a professional Mermaid block diagram showing the full signal-flow architecture of a classic swept-tuned spectrum analyzer. Every block is mapped to the KTU syllabus terminology.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[INPUT RF SIGNAL] --> attnA[Variable Input Attenuator 0 to 70 dB]
    attnA --> lpfA[Low Pass Preselector Filter]
    lpfA --> mixA[RF Mixer]
    loA[Sweeping Local Oscillator VCO] --> mixA
    mixA --> ifA[IF Amplifier and RBW Filter 10 Hz to 3 MHz]
    ifA --> detA[Envelope Detector Log or Linear]
    detA --> vbfA[Video Bandwidth Filter 1 Hz to 10 MHz]
    vbfA --> vA[Vertical Amplifier]
    vA --> crtA[Display Y axis Amplitude in dBm]
    sweeA[Sweep Generator Ramp Voltage] --> loA
    sweeA --> crtA
    crtA[Display] --> outA[Output X axis Frequency]
```

**Block-by-Block Interpretation:**

- **Input RF Signal** — the unknown signal under test entering the analyzer through a $50\,\Omega$ N-type connector.
- **Variable Input Attenuator** — protects the mixer from overdrive; reference level is set by adjusting this attenuator.
- **Low-Pass Preselector** — rejects image frequencies $f_{image} = f_{s} + 2 f_{IF}$ that would otherwise create false displays.
- **RF Mixer** — the heart of the analyzer; multiplies input with LO to produce $f_{IF}$.
- **Sweeping Local Oscillator (VCO)** — a YIG-tuned oscillator whose frequency is linearly swept by the ramp generator.
- **IF Amplifier & RBW Filter** — provides selectivity; the bandwidth of this filter defines the resolution.
- **Envelope Detector** — extracts the amplitude envelope of the IF signal; logarithmic detector gives dB-linear display.
- **Video Bandwidth Filter** — low-pass post-detection filter; reduces noise on the trace.
- **Sweep Generator** — the same ramp voltage drives both the LO frequency AND the X-axis of the display, ensuring frequency calibration.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Spectrum Analyzer Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Need to measure a signal] --> q1{What is the input frequency range?}
    q1 --> lowA[Audio or low RF 0 to 100 kHz]
    q1 --> highA[RF Microwave or mm wave 100 MHz to 50 GHz]
    lowA --> q2{Is the signal transient or continuous?}
    q2 --> contA[Continuous signal] --> fftA[Use FFT Spectrum Analyzer]
    q2 --> transA[Transient or pulse signal] --> rtsaA[Use Real Time Spectrum Analyzer]
    highA --> q3{Do you need to see narrowband details?}
    q3 --> yesA[Yes need high RBW selectivity] --> sweptA[Use Swept Tuned Superheterodyne]
    q3 --> noA[No broadband overview is enough] --> fftA
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common question asks "Which analyzer for a $1\,\text{kHz}$ audio harmonic measurement?" The correct answer is **FFT Spectrum Analyzer**, because at $1\,\text{kHz}$ the A/D converter easily digitizes the signal, and FFT provides fast, phase-coherent analysis. A swept-tuned analyzer would have a disproportionately long sweep time at such low frequencies.

## 4.3 State Diagram: How the Display Trace is Built

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateA[Wait for sweep trigger] --> stateB[LO frequency f_LO set to f_start]
    stateB --> stateC[IF filter centered at f_IF mix input with LO]
    stateC --> stateD[Detect amplitude at this LO frequency]
    stateD --> stateE[Plot amplitude on display at X equals f_start plus IF]
    stateE --> stateF{Is LO at f_stop?}
    stateF -- No --> stateG[Increment LO frequency by delta f]
    stateG --> stateC
    stateF -- Yes --> stateH[Sweep complete trace returns to start]
    stateH --> stateA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Spectrum Analyzer. List its main types. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **spectrum analyzer** is an electronic instrument that displays the amplitude of an input signal as a function of frequency, thereby revealing the spectral (frequency-domain) content of the signal.

**Main Types:**
1. **Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne) Spectrum Analyzer** — uses a sweeping local oscillator and a fixed IF for narrowband analysis.
2. **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) Spectrum Analyzer** — digitizes a block of the time-domain signal and computes the DFT.
3. **Real-Time Spectrum Analyzer (RTSA)** — captures and displays the entire spectrum simultaneously with no sweep blind time.

---

### Question 2: What is Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)? How does it affect the displayed spectrum? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** is the $3\text{-dB}$ bandwidth of the intermediate frequency (IF) filter inside the spectrum analyzer. It determines the analyzer's ability to resolve two closely spaced signal frequencies as distinct peaks.

**Effects on the displayed spectrum:**
- **Smaller RBW** → narrower IF filter → finer frequency resolution, lower noise floor, but **longer sweep time** and slower display update.
- **Larger RBW** → wider IF filter → faster sweeps but **poor resolution** (closely spaced signals merge) and higher noise floor.

The trade-off is: $T_{sw} \propto \dfrac{Span}{RBW^{2}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a swept-tuned superheterodyne spectrum analyzer. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Input Attenuator (1 mark):** The incoming RF signal is first passed through a variable attenuator (typically $0$ to $70\,\text{dB}$ in $10\,\text{dB}$ steps) to prevent damage to the mixer and to set the reference level. The reference level is the amplitude corresponding to the top graticule line of the display.

2. **Low-Pass Preselector (1 mark):** A low-pass filter at the input rejects image frequencies (calculated as $f_{image} = f_{s} \pm 2 f_{IF}$) which would otherwise appear as false signals. This is essential in single-conversion superheterodyne designs.

3. **Mixer and Local Oscillator (2 marks):** The mixer is a non-linear device (typically a double-balanced diode mixer) that multiplies the input signal with the output of a sweeping local oscillator (YIG-tuned oscillator). The mixing law is:
$$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{s} \vert$$
The LO is swept linearly across the desired frequency range by a ramp voltage from the sweep generator.

4. **IF Amplifier and RBW Filter (1.5 marks):** The IF stage (typically at $21.4\,\text{MHz}$ or $321.4\,\text{MHz}$ in modern analyzers) provides the bulk of the gain and contains the resolution-bandwidth filter. RBW is selectable from $1\,\text{Hz}$ to $3\,\text{MHz}$ in a $1{-}3{-}10$ sequence.

5. **Envelope Detector and Video Filter (1 mark):** The IF signal is envelope-detected (logarithmic detector preferred for dB display) and then smoothed by the video bandwidth (VBW) low-pass filter ($1\,\text{Hz}$ to $10\,\text{MHz}$). VBW $\le$ RBW to avoid smearing.

6. **Display (0.5 marks):** The detected signal drives the Y-axis (amplitude in dBm), while the same ramp that drives the LO also drives the X-axis (frequency). The trace on the CRT/LCD shows amplitude vs. frequency.

**(b) Explain the following controls/facilities of a spectrum analyzer: (i) Frequency Span, (ii) Resolution Bandwidth, (iii) Video Bandwidth, (iv) Reference Level. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Frequency Span (1.5 marks):**
Span is the width of the frequency window displayed on the screen, defined as $Span = f_{stop} - f_{start}$. It is the total range swept by the LO during one sweep. Span can be set from a few Hz up to the full range of the analyzer. A wide span gives an overview of the spectrum; a narrow span zooms into fine detail.

**(ii) Resolution Bandwidth (2 marks):**
RBW is the $3\text{-dB}$ bandwidth of the IF filter and determines the smallest frequency difference between two signals that can be resolved as separate peaks. If two signals are closer than the RBW, they appear merged. RBW is also the dominant factor in the noise floor: $DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF$. Halving RBW lowers the displayed noise floor by $3\,\text{dB}$.

**(iii) Video Bandwidth (2 marks):**
VBW is a low-pass filter placed after the envelope detector. It smooths the displayed trace by averaging noise fluctuations. Setting $VBW = RBW/10$ or $RBW/100$ cleans up noisy traces, especially at narrow RBW settings. The trade-off is that very small VBW values cause slow trace update rates.

**(iv) Reference Level (1.5 marks):**
The reference level is the amplitude (in dBm) assigned to the top graticule line of the display. It is set by adjusting the input attenuator and IF gain such that the signal of interest appears at a convenient vertical position. Modern analyzers auto-couple the attenuator and IF gain when the reference level is changed.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an FFT spectrum analyzer. Compare it with a swept-tuned spectrum analyzer. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inB[Input Signal] --> aaB[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    aaB --> adcB[High Speed Analog to Digital Converter]
    adcB --> memB[Memory Buffer]
    memB --> dspB[DSP Processor computes FFT]
    dspB --> magB[Magnitude Calculation sqrt of I squared plus Q squared]
    magB --> logB[Log Amplitude Conversion dB]
    logB --> vB[Video Filter]
    vB --> dispB[Display Amplitude vs Frequency]
```

**Working Explanation (2 marks):**
The input signal is first low-pass filtered to satisfy the Nyquist criterion ($f_{sample} \ge 2 f_{max}$). A high-speed ADC digitizes a finite time record of the signal. The digitized samples are stored in memory and processed by a DSP chip that computes the Discrete Fourier Transform using the Cooley-Tukey FFT algorithm in real time. The complex FFT output (real and imaginary parts) is converted to magnitude and then to a logarithmic scale for dB display. The result is plotted as amplitude vs. frequency.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Swept-Tuned | FFT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency range | Up to 50 GHz and beyond | Limited by ADC sample rate (typically < 1 GHz) |
| Sweep time | Slow at narrow RBW | Fast (one block acquisition) |
| Phase information | Lost (envelope detection) | Preserved (complex FFT output) |
| Best for | Narrowband RF signals | Wideband baseband/audio signals |
| Cost & complexity | Lower for basic RF | Higher (DSP-intensive) |
| Sensitivity | Excellent ($-170$ dBm typical) | Moderate (quantization noise) |

**(b) Explain the following terms with respect to spectrum analyzers: (i) Sensitivity and noise floor, (ii) Phase noise, (iii) Image frequency, (iv) Shape factor. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Sensitivity and Noise Floor (2 marks):**
The sensitivity of a spectrum analyzer is its ability to detect weak signals. It is quantified by the **Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL)**, given by:
$$DANL = -174\,\text{dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF$$
The noise floor is the baseline of random noise visible on the display when no signal is present. A signal can only be measured if its amplitude is at least $3$ to $10\,\text{dB}$ above the noise floor. Reducing RBW lowers the noise floor and improves sensitivity.

**(ii) Phase Noise (1.5 marks):**
Phase noise is the short-term random fluctuation in the phase of the local oscillator, which appears on the display as a "skirt" or "pedestal" of noise around an otherwise sharp carrier. It is specified in $\text{dBc/Hz}$ (dB below carrier per Hz of bandwidth) at a given frequency offset. Phase noise masks nearby weak signals, so for adjacent-channel measurements, a low-phase-noise LO is essential.

**(iii) Image Frequency (1.5 marks):**
In a superheterodyne receiver, the image frequency is the spurious input frequency that also produces the same IF as the desired signal. For a desired signal $f_s$, the image is:
$$f_{image} = f_{s} + 2 f_{IF}$$
The preselector low-pass filter at the input must attenuate the image by at least $70\,\text{dB}$ to avoid false displays. Modern analyzers use upconversion to a high first IF (e.g., $321.4\,\text{MHz}$) to push the image far out of the input band.

**(iv) Shape Factor (2 marks):**
The shape factor of the IF filter is the ratio of its $60\text{-dB}$ bandwidth to its $3\text{-dB}$ bandwidth:
$$SF = \frac{BW_{60dB}}{BW_{3dB}}$$
An ideal rectangular filter would have $SF = 1$, but real filters are rounded. A **Gaussian filter** has $SF \approx 4.6{:}1$, while a **synchronously-tuned 4-pole filter** has $SF \approx 4{:}1$. A lower shape factor means the filter rejects adjacent frequencies better, improving resolution and reducing measurement uncertainty.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Span and RBW:** Many students write "Span is the resolution of the analyzer" — this is wrong. Span is the *display width*; RBW is the *resolution*.
> 2. **Forgetting the $50\,\Omega$ reference:** When converting dBm to voltage, always state that the reference is $50\,\Omega$ (or $75\,\Omega$ for video).
> 3. **Omitting the sweep-time trade-off:** Any RBW-related answer that does not mention sweep time is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 4. **Wrong heterodyne equation:** Writing $f_{IF} = f_{LO} + f_{s}$ (sum instead of difference) loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 5. **Missing the preselector:** In the block diagram, omitting the low-pass pre-filter at the input costs a full mark, because the examiner will assume the student does not understand image rejection.
> 6. **Mixing up dBm and dBV:** dBm is for power; dBV is for voltage. A common error in numerical problems costs easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Spectrum Analyzer** = Amplitude vs. Frequency display of an electrical signal.
- **Three main types:** Swept-Tuned, FFT, Real-Time.
- **Heterodyne Law:** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{s} \vert$ — the foundation of every swept analyzer.
- **RBW** = $3\text{-dB}$ bandwidth of IF filter = frequency resolution capability.
- **VBW** = post-detection smoothing filter; rule of thumb: $VBW \le RBW$.
- **Span** = $f_{stop} - f_{start}$; the width of the displayed frequency window.
- **Sweep Time** $\propto \dfrac{Span}{RBW^2}$ — narrower RBW means MUCH longer sweep.
- **DANL** = $-174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$ — the noise floor in dBm.
- **Image Frequency** = $f_{s} + 2 f_{IF}$ — must be rejected by the preselector.
- **Shape Factor** = $BW_{60dB} / BW_{3dB}$; lower is better.
- **dBm** = power referenced to $1\,\text{mW}$; **dBV** = voltage referenced to $1\,\text{V}_{\text{RMS}}$.
- **Reference Level** = amplitude at the top graticule; set by input attenuator and IF gain.
- **Swept analyzer** excels at high-frequency narrowband RF; **FFT analyzer** excels at low-frequency wideband signals with phase information.
- **Phase noise** is the skirt around a carrier caused by LO instability; measured in $\text{dBc/Hz}$.
- **Always state the input impedance** ($50\,\Omega$ RF / $75\,\Omega$ video) when discussing voltage or power.
- **KTU Top Recurring Themes:** block diagram of swept analyzer, list of controls (span, RBW, VBW, ref level), heterodyne equation, and spectrum-analyzer vs. oscilloscope comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrument Transformers: Current Transformer, Potential Transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEIT304 - SENSORS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instrument Transformers: Current Transformer, Potential Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Instrument Transformers — The Big Picture

In high-voltage and high-current AC power systems (substations, switchgear, transmission lines, industrial drives), it is **physically impossible, unsafe, and economically impractical** to connect measuring instruments (ammeters, voltmeters, wattmeters, energy meters, relays) directly to the line. The voltages may be in the range of **11 kV to 765 kV** and currents may be in **kilo-amperes**.

**Instrument Transformers** are precision, specially-designed transformer-type transducers that isolate the measuring instrument from the high-voltage/high-current circuit, and step the quantity down to a safe, standardized, low value that can be measured by ordinary instruments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Instrument transformers are transformers used in conjunction with measuring instruments for the purpose of measurement (or control) of current, voltage, power, and energy. They also provide isolation between the high-voltage power circuit and the low-voltage measuring circuit.*

There are **two** types of instrument transformers (the focus of this module):

| Type | Quantity Measured | Standard Secondary Output |
|------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Current Transformer (CT)** | Alternating Current (High Value) | **5 A** or **1 A** |
| **Potential Transformer (PT)** / Voltage Transformer (VT) | Alternating Voltage (High Value) | **110 V** or **110/√3 V** |

---

## 1.2 Current Transformer (CT) — Definition

A **Current Transformer (CT)** is a *step-down* transformer (in terms of current magnitude) whose **primary winding is connected in series** with the main power line carrying the high current to be measured, and whose **secondary winding is connected to an ammeter (or the current coil of a wattmeter/relay)** having a very low impedance (ideally a short circuit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** A current transformer is a transformer that is used to measure alternating current, in which the primary winding, having very few turns, is connected in series with the main circuit, and the secondary winding, having many turns, is connected to an ammeter or current coil of measuring instruments. The secondary is designed to operate **near short-circuit conditions** so that the secondary voltage is very small.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Current Divider" of the Power System

Think of a CT as a **tap on a high-pressure water pipe**. The water (current) is flowing at enormous pressure and volume (say, 1000 A). You cannot connect a small domestic flow-meter directly — it would burst. So you install a *current-transformer tap* — essentially a small shunt (the secondary winding) that allows a *proportional tiny current* (5 A) to flow through a delicate meter, while the main current continues unimpeded through the primary.

- The **primary is the main pipe** (often just one turn — sometimes the bus-bar itself passes through the CT core).
- The **secondary is the small measuring tap** (many turns wound on a toroidal core).
- The **ratio is engineered** so that exactly $5\,\text{A}$ (or $1\,\text{A}$) comes out for any primary current within the CT's rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a power transformer, a CT is **NOT** a voltage step-down device. The secondary current is fixed by the primary current (and turns ratio), not by load. The secondary voltage is whatever is required to push the secondary current through the burden — it is **low** because the burden is low.

---

## 1.3 Potential Transformer (PT) — Definition

A **Potential Transformer (PT)**, also called a **Voltage Transformer (VT)**, is a *step-down* transformer (in voltage) whose **primary winding is connected across (in parallel with) the high-voltage line** to be measured, and whose **secondary winding is connected to a voltmeter (or voltage coil of a wattmeter/relay)** having a very high impedance (ideally an open circuit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** A potential transformer is a transformer used to measure high alternating voltages, in which the primary winding, having many turns, is connected across the high-voltage circuit, and the secondary winding, having few turns, is connected to a voltmeter or voltage coil of measuring instruments. The secondary is designed to operate **near open-circuit conditions**, very much like an ordinary small power transformer at no load.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voltage Reducer" of the Power System

Think of a PT as a **step-down nozzle on a fire hydrant**. The hydrant delivers water at lethal pressure (say 11 kV equivalent pressure). You cannot read the pressure with a domestic pressure gauge. So you install a *pressure-reducer* (the PT) — a small device that delivers the same pressure ratio at a safe, measurable level (e.g., 110 V).

- The **primary is the high-voltage input** (many turns, designed to withstand the full line voltage plus BIL — Basic Insulation Level).
- The **secondary is the low-voltage output** (fewer turns, typically giving 110 V or 110/√3 V).
- The **ratio is set** so that when primary = 11,000 V, secondary = 110 V (ratio = 100:1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a CT, a PT behaves like an **ordinary small transformer at no load** — the secondary voltage is roughly constant (set by the turns ratio), independent of the connected burden (as long as burden is within rating). The secondary is **never** short-circuited; doing so would draw destructive current.

---

## 1.4 Why Instrument Transformers? — The Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Reasons Instrument Transformers Are Essential in KTU 2024 Curriculum:**
>
> 1. **Safety** — Personnel and instruments are isolated from lethal voltages.
> 2. **Standardization** — All secondary instruments are designed for **5 A / 110 V**, regardless of the primary quantity.
> 3. **Economy** — A single, cheap 5 A ammeter can be used anywhere; the CT/PT costs scale.
> 4. **Range Extension** — By changing the CT/PT ratio, the same instrument reads any primary value.
> 5. **Multiple Loads** — One CT/PT can feed meters + relays + transducers in parallel/series.
> 6. **Grounding & Isolation** — Secondary can be earthed, providing a stable reference.
> 7. **Power Measurement** — Wattmeters and energy meters need matched, isolated voltage and current inputs.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Ratings (KTU 2024 Highlight)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Secondary Current of CT | **5 A** (common) or **1 A** (preferred for long leads) |
| Secondary Voltage of PT | **110 V** (line-to-line systems) or **110/√3 V ≈ 63.5 V** (line-to-neutral / phase voltages) |
| CT Primary Currents (Typical) | 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 75, 100, … 200, 300, 400, 600, 800, 1200, 1600, 2000, 3000, 4000, **5000 A** |
| PT Primary Voltages (Typical) | 11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV |
| Rated Frequency (India) | **50 Hz** |
| Accuracy Class (Metering) | 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 |
| Accuracy Class (Protection) | 5P, 10P, PS (IEC 61869) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Current Transformer (CT) — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Construction

A CT has the following physical/electrical construction:

- **Core**: Made of high-grade, grain-oriented silicon steel (or **nickel-iron alloy / nanocrystalline / permalloy** for precision metering CTs) to give very high permeability and low core loss. Usually a **closed toroidal (ring-shaped) core** is used for bar-primary CTs.
- **Primary Winding (Np)**: Has **very few turns** — often **Np = 1**. For high-voltage CTs, the primary is just the **bus-bar** that passes *once* through the toroidal core. For low-voltage CTs, a few turns of heavy conductor are wound.
- **Secondary Winding (Ns)**: Has **many turns** of fine enameled copper wire, distributed uniformly around the entire toroidal core. Standard secondary rating is **5 A**.
- **Insulation**: The primary-to-secondary insulation must withstand the full line voltage (typically the full BIL of the system).
- **Terminal Box**: For connecting burden, earthing, and short-circuiting the secondary when not in use (to prevent open-secondary damage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bar-Primary CT:** A very common construction where the *bus-bar itself* acts as the single-turn primary. The toroidal core with the multi-turn secondary is slipped over the bar. This construction is used in switchgear and panel boards up to several thousand amperes.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle — "Shorted-Secondary" Operation

When the primary carries current $I_p$, an alternating magnetomotive force (MMF) of $N_p I_p$ ampere-turns is produced. To maintain flux in the core, the secondary carries current $I_s$ such that:

$$N_p I_p \approx N_s I_s \quad \text{(approximate, ideal, no loss case)}$$

Because the ammeter/burden has **near-zero impedance**, the secondary voltage $E_s = I_s \cdot Z_b$ is very small, and the induced flux in the core is small. Hence the magnetizing current is small, and the equality is very close to the actual case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "shorted-secondary" condition is THE defining operational feature of a CT.** A CT is *designed* to be operated with its secondary terminals connected directly (or through a low-impedance burden) to an ammeter. The secondary must **NEVER be open-circuited** while the primary is energized.

### 2.1.3 The Open-Secondary Hazard (KTU 2024 Frequently Asked)

If the secondary of an energized CT is opened (broken):

1. The secondary current $I_s$ suddenly drops to zero.
2. The demagnetizing MMF $N_s I_s$ disappears.
3. The entire primary MMF $N_p I_p$ now becomes magnetizing MMF.
4. The core flux rises to an enormously high value (limited only by saturation), at **line frequency**.
5. A huge peak flux produces a **dangerously high voltage** across the secondary terminals (can be **hundreds or thousands of volts**, sufficient to kill a person, destroy insulation, and cause arcing).
6. The core saturates and overheats due to high hysteresis and eddy-current losses.
7. The CT may be permanently damaged (residual magnetism, insulation breakdown).

> [!WARNING]
> **CT Safety Rule (KTU Practical Exam Mandatory Knowledge):**
> *Before removing or connecting any instrument from a CT secondary, the secondary must first be SHORT-CIRCUITED using a shorting switch/link provided on the CT terminal block. Only then should the instrument be removed.*

### 2.1.4 Nominal Ratio, Actual Ratio, and Transformation Ratio

**Nominal Ratio ($K_n$):** The ratio marked on the CT nameplate. It is fixed by the turns ratio and chosen as a convenient integer.

$$K_n = \frac{I_{p,\text{rated}}}{I_{s,\text{rated}}} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \quad \text{(ideal, by construction)}$$

For example, a "**100/5 A CT**" has $K_n = 20$.

**Actual Ratio ($R$):** The real ratio of primary current to secondary current during operation.

$$R = \frac{I_p}{I_s}$$

For an **ideal CT** (no losses, no magnetizing current): $R = K_n = N_s/N_p$.

For a **real CT**: $R \neq K_n$ due to the magnetizing current $I_0$ that diverts part of the primary MMF.

**Ratio Error ($\varepsilon_r$):** The percentage deviation of the actual ratio from the nominal ratio:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_r = \frac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100\,\% \approx \frac{N_s I_0}{N_p I_s} \times 100\,\% \;}$$

(For small errors, $R \approx K_n$, so the formula can also be written as $\varepsilon_r \approx \frac{K_n - R}{K_n} \times 100\%$.)

**Phase Angle Error ($\theta$):** The angle (in minutes) between the primary current vector and the *reversed* secondary current vector. Caused by the magnetizing current which has a component in-phase with the flux (and hence lags the secondary current by ~90°).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Sign Convention:** A *positive* ratio error means the secondary ammeter reads **low** compared to the true primary current. A *positive* phase error means the reversed secondary current *leads* the primary current.

---

## 2.2 Potential Transformer (PT) — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Construction

- **Core**: Silicon-steel laminated core, similar to a small power transformer. Sometimes a 5-legged core (for three-phase) to provide a return path for zero-sequence flux.
- **Primary Winding (Np)**: Has **many turns** of fine wire, designed for the full line voltage, with **graded insulation** (more insulation near the line end, less near the grounded end).
- **Secondary Winding (Ns)**: Has **few turns** of slightly heavier wire, designed for ~110 V output.
- **Insulation**: Must withstand the full primary voltage + BIL. Oil-immersed, SF6 gas, or cast-resin (dry-type) insulation is common at higher voltages.
- **Earthing**: One end of the primary and *one end of the secondary* are both earthed (safety + reference for HV system).

### 2.2.2 Working Principle — "Open-Secondary" Operation

The PT is connected across the high-voltage line. With the secondary connected only to high-impedance voltmeters (and voltage coils of wattmeters/relays), the secondary current is very small. The PT operates **essentially at no-load**, like a small step-down power transformer.

$$\frac{V_p}{V_s} = \frac{N_p}{N_s} \quad \text{(ideal, no-load)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "open-secondary" condition is THE defining operational feature of a PT.** A PT is designed to be operated with the secondary connected only to high-impedance voltmeters. The secondary must **NEVER be short-circuited** — this would draw enormous current (limited only by small winding impedance) and destroy the PT.

### 2.2.3 PT Ratio Error and Phase Angle Error

Same definitions as CT, but in terms of voltages:

**Nominal Ratio:**
$$K_n = \frac{V_{p,\text{rated}}}{V_{s,\text{rated}}} = \frac{N_p}{N_s}$$

**Actual Ratio:**
$$R = \frac{V_p}{V_s}$$

**Ratio Error:**
$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_r = \frac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100\,\% \approx \frac{N_p I_0 \cos(\alpha - \beta)}{N_s I_s \cos\beta} \times 100\,\% \;}$$

(For PT, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the no-load and load power-factor angles of the secondary burden.)

**Phase Angle Error:** The angle between the primary voltage and the *reversed* secondary voltage.

---

## 2.3 Burden — The Universal Concept

**Burden** is the total impedance connected to the secondary of the instrument transformer. It is the sum of:
- The impedance of the measuring instrument(s).
- The impedance of the connecting leads.
- The impedance of the relay coils (in protection applications).

Burden is specified in **VA** (apparent power) at the rated secondary current/voltage, e.g., *"15 VA, 5 A, 0.8 pf lagging burden for CT"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Point:** As burden increases, ratio error and phase error both increase. Metering CTs are designed for low, accurate burdens; protection CTs are designed for higher burdens but must maintain linearity up to high fault currents (typically 20× rated).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | CT Nominal Ratio | $K_n = I_{pN} / I_{sN} = N_s / N_p$ | dimensionless | Marked on nameplate |
| 2 | CT Actual Ratio | $R = I_p / I_s$ | dimensionless | Measured at given operating point |
| 3 | CT Ratio Error | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100$ | % | Can be ±ve |
| 4 | CT Phase Error | $\theta$ (in minutes of arc) | min | Between $-I_s$ and $I_p$ |
| 5 | CT Primary MMF | $\text{MMF}_p = N_p I_p$ | AT | Magnetizing source |
| 6 | CT Secondary MMF (counter) | $\text{MMF}_s = N_s I_s$ | AT | Demagnetizing |
| 7 | CT Magnetizing MMF | $\text{MMF}_0 = N_p I_0$ | AT | Causes errors |
| 8 | CT MMF Balance | $N_p I_p = N_s I_s + N_p I_0$ | AT | KTU derivation formula |
| 9 | PT Nominal Ratio | $K_n = V_{pN} / V_{sN} = N_p / N_s$ | dimensionless | Marked on nameplate |
| 10 | PT Actual Ratio | $R = V_p / V_s$ | dimensionless | Measured |
| 11 | PT Ratio Error | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100$ | % | Same sign convention |
| 12 | PT Phase Error | $\theta$ (minutes) | min | Between $-V_s$ and $V_p$ |
| 13 | Burden (CT) | $B = I_s^2 \cdot Z_b$ | VA | Apparent power |
| 14 | Burden (PT) | $B = V_s^2 / Z_b$ | VA | Apparent power |
| 15 | Turns Compensation | $N_s' = N_s + \text{(fractional turn, e.g., } \tfrac{1}{2} \text{)}$ | turns | Used to reduce ratio error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Notes for Numerical Problems:**
>
> - Phase angle error is conventionally given in **minutes of arc** (1° = 60 min). 1 min ≈ 0.00029 rad.
> - Ratio error is conventionally given in **%**.
> - For "Class 0.5" CT, ratio error at rated burden is ≤ 0.5 %.
> - For "Class 0.5" PT, ratio error at rated burden is ≤ 0.5 % and phase error ≤ 20 min.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| Power Substations (11 kV – 765 kV) | CTs/PTs feed metering panels, protection relays, SCADA |
| Generating Stations | Generator CTs and PTs for unit protection, AVR, telemetry |
| Industrial Switchgear | Air-break/SF6 switchgear panels use CTs for O/C, E/F protection |
| Energy Billing | Class 0.2/0.5 CTs + PTs in HT consumer metering |
| Power Quality Analyzers | PTs scale voltage for ADC input; CTs scale current |
| HVDC Converter Stations | DC CTs/PTs (special optical types) for valve control |
| Smart Grids / AMI | IoT sensors receive scaled quantities via instrument transformers |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ratio Error and Phase Error of a Current Transformer (CT)

### 3.1.1 Step 0 — Setup and Nomenclature

Let:
- $N_p$ = number of primary turns
- $N_s$ = number of secondary turns
- $I_p$ = primary current (rms)
- $I_s$ = secondary current (rms)
- $I_0$ = excitation (magnetizing) current drawn by the core (rms)
- $I_m$ = magnetizing component of $I_0$ (in-phase with flux, i.e., 90° lagging $V_s$)
- $I_w$ = core-loss component of $I_0$ (in-phase with $V_s$)
- $E_p$ = primary induced EMF
- $E_s$ = secondary induced EMF
- $Z_b$ = burden impedance (ammeter + leads)
- $\cos\beta$ = power factor of the secondary burden (i.e., pf of $Z_b$)
- $Z_s = r_s + jx_s$ = secondary winding impedance (resistance + leakage reactance)
- $V_s$ = voltage across burden = $E_s - I_s Z_s$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Ampere-Turn (MMF) Balance

The net ampere-turns around the closed magnetic core equals the magnetizing ampere-turns:

$$N_p I_p - N_s I_s = N_p I_0$$

Solving for $I_p$:

$$I_p = \frac{N_s}{N_p} I_s + I_0$$

This is the **fundamental CT equation** (sometimes called the *exact* CT equation).

**Conversion logic:** The primary MMF supplies *both* the secondary counter-MMF (which is what creates the useful secondary current) and the magnetizing MMF that energizes the core. The magnetizing MMF is "wasted" from a measurement standpoint — it is the source of all CT errors.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Actual Transformation Ratio

Divide both sides by $I_s$:

$$\frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} + \frac{I_0}{I_s}$$

The actual ratio $R = I_p / I_s$ is therefore:

$$R = K + \frac{I_0}{I_s}$$

where $K = N_s / N_p$ is the turns ratio.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Ratio Error

The ratio error is defined as:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100\,\%$$

where $K_n$ is the nominal ratio (≈ K if no turns compensation).

Substituting $R = K + I_0/I_s$ and noting that the error is small (so $I_0/I_s \ll K$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_r &= \frac{K - \left(K + \frac{I_0}{I_s}\right)}{K + \frac{I_0}{I_s}} \times 100 \\
&\approx \frac{-\frac{I_0}{I_s}}{K} \times 100 \\
&= -\frac{N_p I_0}{N_s I_s} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** The negative sign appears because the actual ratio is slightly *higher* than the turns ratio (since $I_0 > 0$ always adds), so $R > K$, making the numerator negative.

In magnitude (commonly used in textbooks):

$$\boxed{\;|\varepsilon_r| = \frac{N_p I_0}{N_s I_s} \times 100\,\% \approx \frac{I_0}{K \cdot I_s} \times 100\,\%\;}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Phasor Diagram Construction

Take the secondary current $\vec{I_s}$ as reference, lagging it by the burden pf angle $\beta$ gives $\vec{V_s} = \vec{I_s} \cdot Z_b$. The flux $\vec{\phi}$ leads $\vec{V_s}$ by 90° (in the no-loss ideal case) — actually the flux $\vec{\phi}$ lags $\vec{E_s}$ by 90°; $\vec{E_s} \approx \vec{V_s}$ if $Z_s$ is small. The magnetizing current $\vec{I_0}$ has two components:
- $\vec{I_m}$ in phase with $\vec{\phi}$ (the "magnetizing" or "reactive" component)
- $\vec{I_w}$ in phase with $\vec{V_s}$ (the "core-loss" or "active" component)

The phasor equation in vector form is:

$$\vec{I_p} = K \vec{I_s} + \vec{I_0}$$

Resolving along the direction of $K \vec{I_s}$ and perpendicular to it gives the components of $\vec{I_0}$:
- Component **along** $K \vec{I_s}$: $I_0 \cos(\psi)$ — this causes ratio error.
- Component **perpendicular** to $K \vec{I_s}$: $I_0 \sin(\psi)$ — this causes phase error.

where $\psi$ = angle between $\vec{I_0}$ and $\vec{I_s}$.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Phase Angle Error (Degrees/Minutes)

The phase angle error $\theta$ is small (typically < 1°), so:

$$\theta \approx \tan\theta = \frac{I_0 \sin\psi}{K I_s} \quad \text{radians}$$

Converting to **minutes of arc**:

$$\boxed{\;\theta_{\min} = \frac{I_0 \sin\psi}{K I_s} \times \frac{180 \times 60}{\pi} = 3438 \cdot \frac{I_0 \sin\psi}{K I_s}\; \text{min}}$$

(3440 is also commonly used; the conversion factor is $180/\pi \times 60 = 3437.75$.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Phasor Diagram of CT (Detailed Geometry)

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Reference Axis

Take $\vec{I_s}$ along the positive x-axis. Let the burden have pf angle $\beta$ lagging (typical inductive burden, e.g., ammeter + inductive relay coil).

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Drop Vectors

$$\vec{V_s} = \vec{I_s} \cdot \vec{Z_b}, \quad \text{lagging } \vec{I_s} \text{ by } \beta$$

The induced EMF $\vec{E_s}$ in the secondary winding equals $\vec{V_s} + \vec{I_s}\vec{Z_s}$ (the drop across secondary winding impedance $Z_s$). For simplicity, if $Z_s$ is small, $\vec{E_s} \approx \vec{V_s}$.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Flux Vector

The flux $\vec{\phi}$ **lags** the induced EMF $\vec{E_s}$ by 90°.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Excitation Current

The excitation current $\vec{I_0}$ has:
- A **magnetizing component** $\vec{I_m}$ in phase with $\vec{\phi}$ (lagging $V_s$ by ~90°)
- A **core-loss component** $\vec{I_c}$ in phase with $\vec{V_s}$ (since $P_c \propto V_s^2$)

The angle between $\vec{I_0}$ and $\vec{V_s}$ is the **iron-loss angle** $\alpha$ (typically 10°–30°).

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Primary Current

From MMF balance: $\vec{I_p} = K \vec{I_s} + \vec{I_0}$, where $K = N_s/N_p$.

The vector sum gives the primary current phasor $\vec{I_p}$.

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Identifying the Errors

- **Ratio error** arises from the component of $\vec{I_0}$ **along** $K\vec{I_s}$.
- **Phase error** $\theta$ arises from the component of $\vec{I_0}$ **perpendicular** to $K\vec{I_s}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ratio Error and Phase Error of a Potential Transformer (PT)

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Voltage Equations

The PT primary is connected to the high-voltage line. With the secondary delivering $I_s$ to the burden $Z_b$ at pf $\cos\beta$:

$$V_p = E_p - I_p Z_p \quad \text{(primary side, } Z_p = \text{primary impedance)}$$

$$V_s = E_s - I_s Z_s \quad \text{(secondary side)}$$

$$E_p / E_s = N_p / N_s$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Referred-to-Secondary Equivalent

Referring everything to the secondary side by the turns ratio $K_{ps} = N_p/N_s$:

$$K_{ps} V_p = E_s - I_s Z_s \cdot \left(\frac{Z_p}{K_{ps}}\right) \quad \text{(this becomes messy)}$$

The **standard** derivation uses the "T-equivalent circuit referred to the primary" approach, and resolves the small no-load current $\vec{I_0}$ into components along and perpendicular to the secondary voltage.

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Phasor Diagram of PT

Take the secondary voltage $\vec{V_s}$ as reference. The secondary current $\vec{I_s}$ lags $\vec{V_s}$ by the burden pf angle $\beta$ (for lagging pf burden).

The induced EMF $\vec{E_s}$ leads $\vec{V_s}$ by a small angle (the regulation drop angle):

$$\vec{E_s} = \vec{V_s} + \vec{I_s}\vec{Z_s}$$

The flux $\vec{\phi}$ lags $\vec{E_s}$ by 90°.

The primary induced EMF is $\vec{E_p} = K_{ps} \vec{E_s}$, leading $\vec{E_s}$ by the same angle as $E_s$ leads $V_s$ (it's a scalar multiple, so the angle is the same).

The no-load current $\vec{I_0}$ has:
- Magnetizing component $\vec{I_m}$ in phase with $\vec{\phi}$ (lagging $V_s$ by ~90°)
- Iron-loss component $\vec{I_c}$ in phase with $\vec{V_s}$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Primary Voltage

$$\vec{V_p} = \vec{E_p} - \vec{I_p} \vec{Z_p}$$

The actual ratio is $R = V_p / V_s$, the nominal is $K_n = K_{ps}$.

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Ratio and Phase Error (Final Result)

By geometry of the phasor diagram, with $\vec{V_s}$ as reference:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio Error } \varepsilon_r &= -\frac{N_p I_0 \sin(\alpha + \beta)}{N_s I_s} \times 100\,\% \\
\text{Phase Error } \theta &\approx \frac{N_p I_0 \cos(\alpha + \beta)}{N_s I_s} \times 3438 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\alpha$ = iron-loss angle of the core (angle between $\vec{I_0}$ and the perpendicular to $\vec{V_s}$), and $\beta$ = burden pf angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference from CT:** In CT, the magnetizing current $I_0$ flows in the *primary*, so the error formulas contain $I_0 / I_s$ directly. In PT, the magnetizing current is on the *primary* side, but the burden current flows on the *secondary*. After referring to the same side, both error formulas look similar but with phase shifts due to $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A 500/5 A current transformer has a primary of 3 turns. Calculate:
(a) The nominal ratio.
(b) The number of secondary turns.
(c) The actual ratio and ratio error if the magnetizing current is 1.5 A at rated primary current.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Nominal Ratio:
$$K_n = \frac{I_{pN}}{I_{sN}} = \frac{500}{5} = 100$$

**(b)** Secondary turns:
$$N_s = K_n \times N_p = 100 \times 3 = 300 \text{ turns}$$

**(c)** From the CT equation:
$$I_p = \frac{N_s}{N_p} I_s + I_0 = 100 \cdot I_s + I_0$$

At rated primary current $I_p = 500$ A, $I_0 = 1.5$ A:

$$500 = 100 \cdot I_s + 1.5$$
$$I_s = \frac{500 - 1.5}{100} = \frac{498.5}{100} = 4.985 \text{ A}$$

Actual ratio:
$$R = \frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{500}{4.985} = 100.30$$

Ratio error:
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{K_n - R}{R} \times 100 = \frac{100 - 100.30}{100.30} \times 100 \approx -0.299\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Negative ratio error means the actual ratio is *higher* than nominal. The ammeter reads 4.985 A when the true primary is 500 A. The ratio $500/4.985 = 100.30$ means the CT is "over-turning" slightly. This is typical for an uncompensated CT.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — CT vs PT at a Glance (KTU 2-Mark Cross-Question)

| Feature | Current Transformer (CT) | Potential Transformer (PT) |
|---|---|---|
| Measured quantity | High current (kA) | High voltage (kV) |
| Connection in line | **Series** with line | **Parallel (across)** the line |
| Primary turns | **Few** (often 1, the bus bar) | **Many** (thousands) |
| Secondary turns | **Many** (hundreds) | **Few** (hundreds) |
| Secondary quantity | **5 A or 1 A** | **110 V or 110/√3 V** |
| Secondary operation | **Near short-circuit** (low Z burden) | **Near open-circuit** (high Z burden) |
| Secondary voltage | Small (≈ I_s × Z_b) | Large, ≈ 110 V |
| Magnetizing current | Small but causes error | Small, causes error |
| Errors | Ratio + Phase | Ratio + Phase |
| Open-secondary danger | **YES — lethal voltage!** | NO (open is safe) |
| Short-secondary danger | NO (designed for it) | **YES — destructive current!** |
| Operating analogy | No-load power transformer | Short-circuit power transformer |
| Used in relays | Earth-fault, over-current | Under/over-voltage, distance |
